skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: A lobby or chapel at the western entrance of a church. Sometimes a place of burial, an area reserved for women, or a place where monks could meet female relatives.
A chapel that radiates from an apsidal end of a cathedral or church.
One of a series of chapels which project symmetrically from an apse or ambulatory at the east end of a church.
Areas and spaces within a monument or site.
A chapel dedicated to the Virgin, usually east of the chancel.
A chapel, often attached to or screened off inside a church, endowed for the celebration of masses principally for the repose of the founder?s soul.
A building, room or area, often within or attached to a church or other building, serving as a place of worship. Where a monument, use the Thesaurus of Monument Types.
A frieze that has a convex section.
The middle section, between the architrave and the cornice, of an entablature.
A plan in which a four-sided figure has all its angles at right angles and its opposite sides equal and parallel.
A plan in which the chimney stack backs onto the cross passage between a building's two entrances.
The plan on which a circular building is constructed.
A plan of a structure which has eight sides.
A plan of a house where the buildings are around at least three sides of a quadrilateral courtyard.
A building or structural plan shaped like a T.
A plan type of a hall with two wings and a central porch.
A building or structural plan with a U shape.
A circular plan with a series of cells around a central core, often used for prisons.
A plan in which the front entrance opens into a lobby opposite to an axial chimney stack.
A decorative pattern, as used in tracery or a Gothic arch, featuring six lobes, or foils, separated by cusps.
A plan featuring three foils or lobes radiating outwards from a central point.
A building plan in which the rooms are symmetrically placed.
A house plan with a hall and a single wing.
A building plan in the shape of a cross.
An English seventeenth century house plan consisting of a rectangular block two rooms deep, the rooms sometimes separated by a passage.
A common plan form of large medieval and sub medieval houses, with a hall range flanked by two wings; also found with only one wing.
A building plan consisting of three rooms placed in a straight row.
A building plan that features a hall lying between, and at right-angles to, two projecting wings.
A plan in which a chimney containing back-to-back fireplaces heats two rooms simultaneously by being placed within the dividing wall.
A plan form in which pavilions lead off from a spiral corridor.
A plan featuring a lobby entry, situated at one end of a side wall, which provides access to one room only.
A plan in which the building is two rooms deep but has no central corridor.
A plan type, usually for a church, having a rectangular core with one apsidal end wall.
A medieval plan with the hall situated at one end of the main range.
A nineteenth century plan where two or four wings of a house are constructed at an angle to the core, usually at approximately 45 degrees to the wall of the core building.
A building or structure with a six-sided plan.
A plan with four foils radiating out from a central point.
A building plan where space is unconstrained by partitions.
A building plan in which a passage crosses the axis of the building, often at one end of a hall, with an external door at each end.
A simple, one-roomed structural plan.
A building plan in which the principal fireplace is positioned on the side wall.
A plan of a building where the entrance is in the gable wall.
A plan in which the staircase is placed at the centre of the building.
A plan form of a structure or building.
A plan featuring a doorless entry to a lobby in front of an axial chimney-stack.
A plan in which entry is directly into a room without being situated opposite a fireplace.
Plans of structures, particularly ground plans, and divisions or elements of structures that form part of these plans.
A plan of a building which consists of two or more cells where the chimney is placed on the axis of the building between two rooms and midway between the lateral walls.
A part of a building or structure that is an element of the construction.
A wooden nail, round or square in section, used to fix a joint.

A power source deriving its energy from solid fuel, which is used to boil water in an almost closed vessel, producing steam which moves one or more pistons in tight fitting cylinders, the energy being transmitted by a piston rod to a diversity of devices
A steam engine, often used in the 18th century, which drove a pump to return water back to a waterwheel so that it could be used over and over again.
A Capital with a rolled up cushion-like form on either side creating distinctive volutes.
A style of construction following an established set of criteria, based around the conventional use of a type of column.
A base embellished with horizontal reeds and other mouldings.
A column of the Ionic Order, set upon a base and characterized by its slender form.
An entablature consiting of an architrave, usually a frieze and cornice.
An Ionic base with a lower disc with horizontal fluting or reeding, above which is a torus, also reeded.
A classical order with an Attic base and fluted column. The cornice has dentils and the capital volutes.
A column base featuring a hollow, concave - or scotia - moulding between two tori, which are projecting, convex mouldings.
A component part that is designed deliberately for decorative purposes.
A decorative feature in the style of a flower or plant. Use more specific term.
A long much indented leaf usually curled over at the tip which is a standard form of foliage in classical decoration.
Decorative ornamentation of a formalized lily.
Decorative ornamentation in the form of a ball enclosed within petals.
The lesser stalks springing from the principal stalks, or caules, within the decoration on a Corinthian capital.
A circular stylised ornament, essentially a patera with floral enrichment, associated with classical architecture and occurring on the soffits of coffers, and as the fleuron of the Corinthian abacus.
A style of decorative design consisting of intertwined, geommetrical patterns of leaves, branches, tendrils etc. Often defined as being devoid of human or animal figures.
Decorative ornamentation of a flower or leaf.
A decorative feature in the style of a formalized rose.
A stylised, leafy decorative feature reminiscent of, and sometimes referred to as, honeysuckle
A decorative motif representing a palm leaf. Often painted, incised or applied in relief, can occur singly or as a repeating pattern.
Decorative ornamentation in the form of an upright, stalked trefoil used in Tudor architecture.
Bay-leaf shaped decorative ornamentation, usually adorning mouldings or friezes, often depicted in a garland arrangement.
The area directly in front of a fireplace or the floor of a firecplace itself.
A hearth, normally placed clear of the walls, without a chimney stack or hood above.
A vertical partition used to separate one area from another.
A timber screen, often richly decorated, placed in front of a chancel to support a rood loft.
A recess or cupboard within a church, usually positioned near the altar, used for the storage of sacred vessels.
A recess, often containing shelves and usually fitted with a door or doors, used for the storage of items.
Accessories used in furnishing and embellishing a building or structure.
A room or cupboard usually used for the storage of garments.
A joint with a notch cut into its invisible inner face.
A lap joint which has a notch cut in one side.
A junction whereby two members or elements of an artificial structure or mechanism are joined or fitted together, whether rigidly or hinged.
A timber running above vertical posts, in the place of a wall-plate, within a timber-framed building.
A short timber set across the top of a wall to take the foot of a rafter and an ashlar piece.
A horizontal timber usually at the bottom of a timber framed wall into which posts and studs are tenoned.
A horizontal timber that joins the collars of a series of roof trusses.
A longitudinal timber set on top of a timber frame, brick or masonry wall on which roof-trusses or rafters rest.
A plate in a crown post roof, supported on crown posts and bearing the collars.
A general term given to all horizontal timbers laid on walls or posts in order to support other timbers.
A room intended, and often furnished accordingly, as a venue for the holding of meetings.
Originally a reception room where visitors come come to talk especially in a closed establishment such as a monastery or school. Later used to refer to any room used for entertaining guests.
A parlour in a monastery.
A part of a building joined to the core of the structure and at an angle to it.
A wing of a house used specifically for the storage of household materials and victuals.
An ornamental contour given to the angles or features of a building, whether a projection or a cavity, such as a cornice, an architrave or an astragal.
A narrow timber board or moulding lining the base of an interior wall as a finish between it and and the floor.
A room for baking.
A room in which food is prepared.
A large stone laid horizontally to form the roof of a chamber.
A feature which forms part of a building and has a specific functional usage or decorative form.

A shelter for doves or pigeons, often containing nesting boxes, either free-standing or built into other structures (barns, towers etc.). If a dedicated, free-standing structure, index with MONUMENT TYPE: DOVECOTE.
A short, horizontal brace set between a tiebeam and a wall plate to strengthen the structure.
An inclined timber used to support and provide rigidity to roofs, partitions etc.
A frame to a structure formed by precast concrete beams placed in pairs.
A slender and elegant base usually of Attic type with two tori, two scotiae and two astragals.
A classical order having a bell shaped capital that is decorated with laurel, olive or acanthus leaves from which CAULICOLI emerge. The shaft is usually fluted and the base is often an Attic base.
A circular moulding.
A communal wash area, sometimes a dedicated outbuilding, or facility, such as a basin or trough, used by monks.
An H-sectioned strip of lead used to hold panes of glass in position in stained-glass windows or other leaded lights.
A room in which food is prepared.
The opening at the base of a chimney stack.
An area within a large fireplace where it is possible to sit.
A piece of machinery used to convert energy of one sort (for instance steam) into motive energy.
A stationary winding steam engine positioned at the top of a railway incline for hauling up trains and locomotives.
An enclosed compartment within a building intended for a purpose other than as a thoroughfare.
A room for servants, particularly where they would eat.
A room intended for the pursuit of recreational activities.
A room or rooms containing fitness equipment such as running machines and weights.
A succession of arches on shafts with no openings, attached to and used to enliven a wall.
A series of arches.
A base of the Tuscan Order, featuring a square plinth supporting a large torus with a fillet above.
The lowest part of a structure, such as a pier, or the section of an order on which the shaft of a column sits.
A base embellished with horizontal reeds and other mouldings.
A base of the Roman Doric order with a torus, an astragal and a fillet.
An Ionic base with a lower disc with horizontal fluting or reeding, above which is a torus, also reeded.
A column base featuring a hollow, concave - or scotia - moulding between two tori, which are projecting, convex mouldings.
A slender and elegant base usually of Attic type with two tori, two scotiae and two astragals.
Bases of this order are often referred to as spira and have a double astragal in the middle.
A room set aside for the purpose of reading, such as in a library or a club.
A room for private work, reading etc.
A moulding on a flat or vaulted ceiling, suggesting a structure or forming a pattern derived from vaulting..
A tertiary vault rib, usually employed for decorative purposes, that links one intersection of the main ribs to another.
Room in a building or complex, such as a workhouse, in which work is carried out.
A classical order with metopes and triglyphs in the frieze. Greek Doric has no base and a fluted column but Roman Doric does have a base and may or may not have a fluted column.
A base of the Roman Doric order with a torus, an astragal and a fillet.
A combination of timbers to form a frame placed at intervals and carrying the purlins.
A truss with two curved kerb-principals, which are the short principal rafters rising froma tiebeam to a collar, placed either side of a crown strut.
A female figure used instead of a column, usually supporting an entablature.
A sculptural or decorative representation of a human figure. Includes mythical and fantastical figures such as Cupid, Atlas etc.
Set of three seats for the clergy found in the chancel
A fixed object to sit upon. Use narrower terms where known.
An early washing machine used in bleaching works for washing calico and other textiles.
A mechanical testing machine used to carry out pioneering tests on various materials, particularly cast-iron beams, to determine practical data on their strength and other properties.
A machine that presses apples to extract juice for the production of cider.
A machine used for the purification of water, usually for drinking purposes.
A single piece of apparatus that uses mechanical power for a particular function.
A timber, horse-powered device consisting of a large, rotating drum around which a rope would be wound, hoisting material from a quarry pit, mine shaft etc..
An inclined wooden trough which channels grain evenly from the feed hopper into the eye of the stones in a corn mill.
A hand operated machine for winding cotton and linen thread into balls - invented in 1802.
An early form of powered forging hammer, featuring a cast-iron hammer head fixed to a pivoted wooden arm, introduced circa 1500 and used primarily in the iron industry.
A machine for applying protective size to yarn intended for warp
A signal usually mounted on a gantry, be it mechanical or electronic, to provide guidance to traindrivers on access to sections of track.
A machine used for drawing out and twisting together short fibres to make a yarn or thread. Use more specific term where known.
A simple, wind-driven water pump, also known as a windpump.
A device which automatically controls a particular funtion within a machine or mechanism, such as regulating the gap between millstones in a windmill.
A machine tool for producing flat surfaces and slots on small work pieces by cutting away surplus material.
A machine used for combing wool. Use more specific term where known.
A gauge for measuring the volume of water flowing from a river to machinery using the water for motive power??

A machine tool for producing large flat surfaces on metal objects. It comprises a large work table which slides backwards and forwards along a substantial guide bed, passing under a stationary bridge containing a fixed tool.
A cleansing or separating machine used in a corn mill for removing smut from grain before it is ground.
A device for receiving and sending radio-transmitted signals into the atmosphere.
The primary mechanism within an engine or device that transmits motion, especially rotary motion, to wheels, cogs, turntables etc.
A plant used to sterilize water for use in public bathing establishments, not to be confused with desaliation or purification units for drinking water.
A machine used to burn and desiccate rubbish.
A general name given to a series of machines which successively reduce the diameter of rovings before the final spinning into yarn.
A machine used in the silk industry for applying the final twist to silk threads to make them suitable for weaving into a fabric.
A machine for further attenuating a sliver of cotton after it has been through a draw frame.
A machine used for lifting materials.

A powerful machine comprising a pair of heated cylinders revolving inside a chamber, and used for converting raw rubber into a soft plastic mass by being 'chewed' between knives or teeth projecting from the cylinders.
An engine used for directly winding up coal from deep mines, dating from the 18th century.
A device for raising miners individually from a shaft consisting of two rods, raised and lowered alternately, fitted with small platforms and powered by a waterwheel or steam engine.
A steam generator comprising a pressure vessel, and/or rows of tubes arranged to receive heat from a firegrate.

An apparatus, featuring an ascending and descending platform or compartment, for carrying people or objects from one level or floor to another. This term can include the shaft or well in which such an apparatus operates.
A steam-powered machine used for raising materials such as ore or water from a pit or shaft.
A rotating circular platform operated by machinery to turn a steam locomotive around.

An enclosed structure of brick, stone or metal in which material is heated or burned, or, in a domestic context, food is cooked, through the continuous radiation of heat from its floor, walls or ceiling.
A hydraulic machine used to magnify the stroke of a hydraulic piston. The pulleys and chains of a crane or other device are attached to the jigger.
A machine tool capable of creating a variety of forms and surfaces in materials by way of a spinning cutter positioned over a movable table.
A channel that returns water that has been used to turn a waterwheel back to the river or source from which it came.
A device patented in 1733 for speeding up the weaving of cloth.
A mechanism, either automated or consisting of winches and pulleys, that raises sacks of grain on ropes or chains from one floor of a mill to another, usually through one or a series of trap doors.
A large structure, usually associated with power stations, in which hot water undergoes a process of cooling before being re-used .
A mechanical component or device that can change the direction of motion of a pump rod from the vertical to the horizontal plane and vice versa.
A machine used in the worsted industry for cleaning woollen slivers after gilling and cording.
Loading machinery tipping coal into railway wagons, usually at a pithead.
A kiln in which Iron ores were heated before smelting in order to drive off the impurities such as sulphur.
A machine for making paper in a continuous length.

A device in which a large quantity of low pressure water is made to pump a small proportion of itself to a greater height than its source or to a higher pressure; achieved by using the pressure rise resulting from suddenly interrupting the flow
A wheel, usually steam driven, used for raising water by means of buckets attached to its circumference.
A device used in the production of hard cheeses that exerts pressure upon fresh curds in order to remove and drain away excess whey.

A device or engine powered by one or more horses or donkeys walking around a circular track and harnessed so as to turn a large toothed wheel, either in the same plane or above, which turned smaller wheels operating machinery.
A machine for beating out or separating grain from corn.
A machine consisting of a continuously moving band of fabric, rubber, or metal used for transporting people or objects from one place to another.
A slope, usually with rails, along which a vehicle can be moved between two relatively flat stretches of railway, or by which a boat can be transferred from one level of waterway to another.
A horse-powered apparatus, for the crushing of commodities such as ore or cider apples, consisting of a stone wheel running over a circular iron or stone bed.
Machines for automatically feeding fuel onto the fire of steam boilers and other furnace plant, introduced at the beginning of the 19th century.
A machine used for weighing objects.
A mechanical device, used in a corn mill, that sieves and grades flour or meal and separates oversized particles.
A machine for cleansing flax, hemp or cotton and forming the fibres into a continuous wide lap or sheet in preparation for carding.
Apparatus using mechanical power for a particular function.

A machine for weaving cloth. A traditional loom comprises a framework with a horizontal back roller, the warp beam or warp roller, around which closely spaced warp threads have been wound parallel to each other, stretched horizontally onto a front roller.
A vertical or inclined plane, channel, or passage through which objects are moved from a higher to a lower level by means of gravity.
A machine that uses liquid pressure to exert a large force on two platens so that items placed between them are pressed to a shape, compressed, or forced together.
A channel, usually placed upstream of a mill, that carries water from a river or other water source to a waterwheel.
A wheel turned by water to generate mechanical power.
A power driven machine or device for raising a liquid or forcing it along a pipe, or for compressing a gas.
A pulley running on the outside of a belt to improve the effectiveness of a drive.
A large wheel, often of timber construction, rotated by the walking action of people or animals placed within it usually to provide a power source for another machine or device.

Staircase whose moving steps are driven mechanically whilst remaining permanently in a horizontal plane allowing users or objects to be moved effortlessly from one level to another, within or outside a building.
A tank within a salt works used for storing brine after is has been pumped from underground. The brine is then fed by gravity into the evaporating pans in the Pan House.
A device for hoisting, or operating pumps, using water displacement as a lifting and balancing medium.
A series of water or engine-driven crushing machines used in ore dressing.
A machine, usually contained in a building called a flour mill which reduces grain into constituent parts or �streams� which include flour. For a Flour Mill buliding use FLOUR MILL in Monument Type.
A machine for the final reduction in cross sectional area of a cotton sliver into a fine roving preparatory to spinning high counts of yarn.
A motor in which rotary motion is produced by motive power, usually of water, steam or gas, impinging directly on a series of vanes upon the circumference of a revolving cylinder or disc.
A counter balance designed to support the excess weight of a mineshaft's wooden beams.
An elevator used to raise locomotives.
A musical instrument that produces sound through the passage of air through a number of pipes controlled by stops, pedals and keyboards.
A machine tool similar in general appearance to a shaping machine but in which the head or ram carrying the cutting tool moves in a vertical plane.
A cylindrical device using ceramic or metallic balls to grind materials like ores, chemicals, ceramic raw materials and paints
A latrine.
A room intended for, and usually equipped to aid, the performance of ablutions.
A room with apparatus for washing the hands and face, now often combined with a water closet.
A room intended to contain a bath and often other toilet facilities.
A room containing a toilet, water closet etc..
A staircase that rises through only one storey and has treads and risers framed into square studs, one of them often on one side of a door-frame; it usually occupies a rectangular space.
A flight of steps leading from one level to another.
A narrow timber board or moulding lining the base of an interior wall as a finish between it and and the floor.
A vertical timber resting on a hammer beam and forming a triangle between it and a principal.
A substantial vertical member, usually a component of a main framework.
A room, usually in a hospital, in which a number of beds are located.
An area of a building or complex, such as a workhouse or hospital, for isolating new residents before admission into main building, used to prevent spread of disease
A long room for recreation, often on an upper floor.
A long, high-ceilinged room, usually extending the full length of an Elizabethan or Jacobean house and often occupying an upper floor, used as a promenade, place of entertainment etc..
A room within a house or building used to exhibit artworks, often decorated and lit accordingly.
A loft, balcony or gallery, in a hall or church, for the use of musicians.
A rectangular recess along the edge of a timber to receive a shutter, door or window.
A crane, usually used in engineering workshops that can be moved from place to place by means of overhead rails..
A crane, usually used in engineering workshops, that can be moved from place to place, often by means of rails.
A large, convex moulding, sometimes ornamented, set at the base of a column and above its plinth.
A room for storing guns.
A room set aside for the storage of goods or valuables. Use more specific term where known.
A joint which has a splay interrupted by a step
A platform scale set within, and flush with the surface of, a roadway, for weighing vehicles, cattle etc.
A latrine, or privy, situated at the rear of a monastery dormitory.
A representation of a winged boy who represents Cupid or Love.
An open balcony or gallery extending along the outside of a building, providing principal rooms with shade by means of a roof or canopy supported by posts or light columns.
A buttress shaped like an arch and springing from a pier in arched form to the wall.
A mass of stone or brick built against or as part of a wall to provide additional strength to it.
A base on which a statue, column etc. stands.
The lowest part, usually projecting, of a pedestal or the base of a column.
A moulded ring around a shaft.
The part of a column between the capital and the base.
A chamber where discussions of issues take place, often found at universities.
A box or cupboard enclosing the fuses for the electrical circuits within a building.
A horizontal member dividing a window into two or more lights.
A room where usually materials or consumables are processed into tools or meal, for example in a workshop or mill.
An area or space within a building.
Part of a church in front of the choir and usually enclosed by tombs, screens etc.
Part of a large church, east of the nave, appropriated for singers and clergy.
The space between the high altar and the east wall or chapel in a large church, and often the area west of a Monks' choir.
One of a building's horizontal divisions, being the space between two floors, or a floor and the roof structure.
A booth, cubicle or recess, often enclosed, in which a priest, usually screened from view, hears confessions.
A passageway within a building providing access to rooms.
An open area within an exchange building (e.g. cotton exchange, corn exchange, stock exchange) where goods are traded.
The area where the act of Offertory, where bread and wine are placed upon the altar, takes place.
A room in which public or private auctions are held.
A part of the chancel of a church where the altar is situated.
The part of the chancel, between the altar rail and the eastern wall, containing the high altar.
A small chamber or private room.
A narrow means of access, often internal.
A large division of a building which lies across, and at right angles to, its main axis, such as the arms either side of the crossing in a cruciform church.
The body of a church or chapel as opposed to galleries.
The partially enclosed western part of a chapel, leading to the chapel proper.
A central area within a building.
An underground, or partially underground, vault or crypt beneath a church or other building.
A clear area in a church covered by the transepts and crossing.
The intersection and space between the nave, chancel and transepts in a cruciform church.
A small private chapel in either a house or church.
An area in a church, sometimes defined by screens, containing a shrine. Also a shrine, usually portable, containing religious relics.
Found in early churches this was a part of a church on the south side of the chancel where there were bookshelves and storage for vestments.
The area that contains the ambulatory, apse and chapels. Usually found in larger churches, monasteries and cathedrals.
The area of the Basilica, especially in the early church, in which the altar resides.
A stage in a church tower where bell ringers stand.
The central division of a church, extending from the west end to the chancel or transepts.
An area normally below a stage or dais occupied by an orchestra or musical group for performances and entertainment.
The choir and sanctuary in the liturgical eastern part of a church appropriated for those who officiate during services.
A roofed structure, open on one side, usually attached to a building, providing a seating area and its occupants with an uninterrupted exterior view.
The consecrated ground immediately surrounding a tomb or grave.
An area or wing of a hexagonal plan workhouse or prison from where the supervision of the inmates is carried out.
An apartment or suite of rooms usually occupying the top floor of a building.
Manmade structure that contains water that is heated to a high temperature and sometimes has bubbling air jets. Meant to be used for therapeudic purposes.
A heated room within a monastery where monks could warm themselves.
A loft for the storage of hay.
A dedicated room for housing the organ feeder bellows, can be an underground chamber or a free-standing building.
A bay in a two storeyed house open to the roof to allow smoke to escape.
A building, or part of a church, used for baptismal rites, usually containing a font.
A mechanical device, used in a corn mill, that separates bran from meal to produce a whiter flour.
A porch in front of the solid wall of a cloister.
A space or area that is outside a building.

A decorative feature of wood, plaster or carved masonry, often used in low relief on ceilings, panels or screens and resembling interlacing leather straps. Popular in the late 16th and early 17th centuries.
An architrave surrounding a door, window or niche interrupted by large blocks of stone, and often by a massive keystone set under a pediment. Named after the architect James Gibbs (1682-1754).

The lowest of the three main parts of an entablature. Also the lintels, jambs and mouldings surrounding a door, window, panels or niche; or the ornamental mouldings around the exterior curve of an arch.

A pointed arch which, on a plan, is shown to be constructed around four arcs: two centred below and two centred upon the springing line, which is the level at which the vertical sides of the arch begin to curve.
A curved construction, spanning an opening, formed from wedge-shaped blocks supported by mutual pressure; they can also be formed from moulded concrete and cast metal.
A half gable or half pediment concealing the lean-to roof of an aisle.
A gable with sides shaped like a flight of steps.
A gable with curved sides and an identifiable pediment.
A gable without a roof behind it.
The part of the wall that supports a pitched roof at the end of the roof.
A small gable above a hipped roof. Also used ornamentally, such as on a buttress or over a niche.
A gable supporting a roof, placed at right-angles to the main part of the roof.
A hole occupied by a timber post or the void remaining after its removal, usually with packing.
Terms covering features of a monument or building which imply the previous presence of a component or structure. Use for negative evidence of a component or structure.
The space occupied by or left where a post has been.

A short, tapered, vertical stone supporting a rough stone disc so resembling a toadstool, often forming one of several uprights on which a timber structure, such as a granary rests, protecting the building from damp and vermin.
A shaped or decorated raised panel below the sill of a window or a niche.
An 18th century hand-operated machine capable of spinning several yarns at once, invented in circa 1767.
The lower, or inner, curve of an arch
A steam engine that uses the same steam successively to drive pistons in high and then low pressure cylinders.
A high speed, single acting compound steam engine with centrally placed piston valves controlling the distribution of steam.
A dining hall, usually in a monastery or college.
A room, furnished with tables and chairs, where meals and refreshments are served and consumed.
A walkway around the parapets of a castle or the roof of a church.

An engine or prime mover, in which a mixture of air and fuel is ignited inside a closed cylinder, and the resulting explosion drives a piston to the opposite end of the cylinder, its movement turning a crankshaft via suitable mechanical parts.
Internal combustion engine powered by diesel fuel.
An internal combustion engine which uses a mixture of air and petrol as fuel, the combustion of which is by an electric spark.
An oil-fuelled internal combustion engine, developed in the late 19th century, and usually made as a stationary, horizontal unit.
An internal combustion heat engine which uses gas as its fuel, and acts as a prime mover or power source for driving other machinery.
A small slab in a Doric cornice.
The upper part of the classical entablature. Also a moulded projection crowning a door, wall, window etc.
A waterwheel where the inflow of water strikes the paddles, or floatboards, below the axis of the wheel.
An undershot waterwheel, developed by General Jean Victor Poncelet (1788-1867) in France, which improved efficiency through the replacement of flat, wooden paddles with curved, metal vanes.
A moulding on an entablature that is between the frieze and the corona.
Entry to a hall through opposing doors with a partition screening the hall and forming a passageway.
An early design of pump featuring a series of pistons, or sometimes bundles of rags, passing through a barrel on a continuous chain, forcing water upwards.
A thin wooden slab or tile used in quantity as a roof or wall covering.
A mullion which divides a window with four or more lights and is larger than the mullions either side of it, often sharing the dimensions of the frame?s members.
A mullion of square section set diagonally.
A vertical member dividing a window into two or more lights.
An atrium or open court, surrounded by porticoes, at the west end of a church.
A buttress placed at the exterior corner of a building, forming a 135 degree angle with the walls it supports.
A room intended to contain a bed.
A room for personal rather than public use.
A small, finely-furnished room, traditionally where a lady could retire to be alone or entertain intimate friends.
A sleeping apartment usually containing a number of beds and sometimes partitioned into cubicles.A room for sleeping in, usually for a number of beds.
A room used for sleeping or resting in. Use more specific term.
A decorative tile on the end of the cornice or eaves of a building to conceal the ends of tiles.
A purlin that is joined to a principal rafter by a tenon joint.
A longitudinal member in a roof, running beneath the collar beams and intended to give stability to the roof.
A purlin that passes a principal rafter
A horizontal timber set in the plane of a roof's slope and supporting common rafters.
A staircase, circular in plan, that winds around a central newel.
The place, hall or chamber in which justice is administered.
A joint between two timbers meeting end to end.
An arch made up of part of a circle, the centre of which is below its springing line.

A propulsion system for ships which need a maximum speed that is considerably faster than their cruise speed, particularly warships. It consists of diesel engines for cruising and gas turbines for high-speed transits.
A room, within a building or institution, equipped for conducting scientific experiments or research.
A decorative S-shaped moulding, usually placed at the top of a cornice, with a concave lower half and a convex upper half.
The top part of a column, often decorated.
A capital which is decorated with stylized leaves similar to small volutes.
A brace halved to the inside of studs, and so not visible from outside.
The warm room in a Roman baths.
A screen separating the choir from other parts of the church.
A keystone decorated with a relief sculpture, usually a cartouche
A wedge shaped brick used to construct arches.
The wedge-shaped block placed at the crown of an arch to consolidate the structure. Sometimes employed as a decorative feature.
A workroom or apartment, often with a north-light, used for the creation of artwork by painters, sculptors etc.
A small form of spire.
A tall, circular, polygonal or square structure rising from a roof, tower, turret etc., tapering to a slender point.

A room, particularly in a house or theatre, intended for dressing or changing one's clothes and furnished with wardrobes, dressing tables and other facilities for the application of make-up, styling of hair etc.
Describes a Classical interior, exterior or facade without pilasters or columns.
The external face or elevation of a building, especially the principal front.
A principal member in a cruck truss.

A pair of timbers, or blades, usually curved, rising from a place at or near ground level to a point at or near the apex of a roof, forming an approximately triangular frame on which the subsidiary structure rests.
Decorative ornamentation in the form of a horn filled with fruit etc.
A large gearwheel which, together with a smaller gear called a pinion, connects two parrallel shafts and, in a corn mill, drives the stone nuts.
The hot room in a Roman baths.
A feature formed by water falling from the roof of a building and creating a linear or curving indentation in the ground.
A cruck truss whose blades are composed of two pieces of timber joined near the wall head in a variety of ways.
In the Classical Orders, the horizontal assembly of architrave, frieze and cornice carried upon columns and pilasters.
An entablature of the Tuscan Order consisting of a plain architrave, frieze and cornice without decoration or enrichments..
A steam engine working an underground cable haulage system in a late 19th century coal mine.
A truss of slighter construction between principal trusses in an elaborate roof.
Room in a building or complex in which the master of an establishment, such as a workhouse or a school, is accommodated.
An inclined member used to support laths under a roof covering. Normally one of a pair.
A rafter set at the corner of a hipped roof, to take the upper end of a common rafter.
A gallery or platform within a place of worship housing the organ.
An upper story in a chapel, church or cathedral, situated above the aisle and below the clerestory, often arcaded and sometime called a Tribune.
The exterior upper covering, and its supporting structure, of a building.
A roof with one side longer than the other, continuing at the same pitch over an extension to a building. Commonly found in New England Colonial architecture.
A lean-to or monopitched roof, or a projecting canopy over a door, window, or stair.
A shaft constructed to allow the circulation of fresh air, into a mine, building etc.
A tall structure of any form, high in proportion to its lateral dimensions, often rising in stages, freestanding or part of another building, often used in fortifications and churches.
A small tower, usually round or polygonal and attached to a structure. When a separate building, treat as a monument.
A small turret that projects from the top of a tower or parapet.
A small or subordinate tower, normally forming part of a larger structure, housing a spiral or winding stair.
A turret on the side of a house providing views across the surrounding countryside.
A horizontal member spanning an opening and providing support to the wall above the opening.
A lintel over a window.
A large defensive area that usually has to be crossed before entering the inner bailey of a castle.
Defences or an external wall surrounding a castle's motte or keep. Also a castle's outer court, or a court within a castle's walls.
Glass that has been coloured by fusing glass with a metallic oxide film or by burning pigment into the surface.
A chamfer with a convex bead-like moulding projecting from the chamfer.
A surface formed by cutting off a square edge, usually at 45 degrees. Use more specific type where known.
A chamfer that has been hollowed out to form a concave shape. This is also known as a chamfret.
A chamfer which does not extend the whole length of the surface it is ornamenting. The stop is the end of the feature and can be simple or ornamented.
Two intersecting beams set on the ground to support a post, usually for a post mill.
An area partly or fully within the roof space of a building.
Space used for storage in the roof.
The wood, stone or brick framework, lining a doorway, from which the door is hung.
A form of entrance closed by a gate.
The space in which a door is placed, allowing passage through.
An entrance large enough for carriages and vehicles leading through a building or wall into an inner courtyard.
A small opening built into a stone wall or hedge to allow small animals through.
The passage, area, lobby, room or vestibule into which a building's main entrance opens.
The entry to a hall through opposed doorways, but without a passage formed by a wall or partition.
A passage, usually horizontal, leading into a mine to allow for access or drainage.
A place of entry into a site, monument or building.
A small shaft or column.
A whole male figure used in place of a column to support an entablature.
A polygonal or circular stone forming part of the shaft of a column.
A column at the corner of a structure.
A vertical structure usually with an Attic base or spira with slender and elegant design, with a capital decorated with acanthus leaves and an entablature rich in carved ornament.
A square pillar.
A column of the Tuscan Order featuring a plain, unfluted shaft.
An engaged column, that is one attached to a wall.
A small, flat band or fillet around a shaft or column.
A detached, sometimes monolithic vertical structural element usually circular, square or polygonal bearing an ornate eight-voluted Ionic Capital.
A column whose shaft is interrupted by square blocks.
A female figure used instead of a column, usually supporting an entablature.
An unengaged, or free-standing, column.
A column that is equidistant, with intercolumniations, even at the corners, with two or more triglyphs over each intercolumniation.
The outward curve at the top or bottom of a column where it joins the base or capital.
A pier made up of several columns clustered together. They can be attached through their whole length or only at top and bottom.
A column joined to another by a band or ring.
A column whose shaft is scuplted to give it a contorted or twisted appearance, sometimes referred to as a barley-sugar column.
A column in which the shaft is interrupted by stone bands.
A vertical, circular member, usually load-carrying. Where separate, use monument type.
A column of the Ionic Order, set upon a base and characterized by its slender form.
A column that is attached to or partly sunk into a wall or pier.
Niche intended for pigeons or other birds to nest in, allowing the squabs and eggs to be harvested. Found inside specially built dovecotes and in the walls of other buildings.
The space occupied by or left where a post has been.
A hole occupied by a timber post or the void remaining after its removal, usually with packing.
An item used to fasten, bind or join one item to another.
A metal binding or ring fastened around the top of a pile to stop splitting.
The vertical face of a step.
A series of flat-topped structures, usually wooden or stone, on which a person places their feet when ascending or descending, facilitating movement from one level to another.
A member whose purpose is to keep two other members apart, so acting in a state of compression.
An upright timber similar to a crown post, but not supporting a plate.

An engine which operates machinery via a pivoted, horizontal beam with a piston located at one end and a reciprocating pump beneath the other, resulting in a rocking, seesaw-like motion. Variations featured the steam piston placed above the beam.

A design of beam steam engine introduced circa 1800 in which, instead of a single overhead rocking beam, there are two beams positioned alongside the cylinder, one either side, giving a lower overall height.
A room or cupboard usually used for the storage of garments.
A bay window, supported by corbelling, cills or brackets, projecting from an upper storey of a building.
An opening in a wall, roof, door, etc to allow light to enter. Usually used to include all component parts.
A major horizontal timber spanning a roof space to connect a pair of principal rafters and prevent them spreading.
A tiebeam whose central section is omitted to increase head room; its inner ends are held by a slip-braces or struts rising from a dropped tiebeam and can be used in end frames.

A bladed wheel attached to the rear, and at right angles to the main sails, of a windmill. Connected to winding gear, the fantail would catch the wind when it changed direction, allowing the mill to move automatically into the wind.
A room where a monarch or similar would meet people, usually in a palace or similar building.
A capital at the corner of a colonade or portico in the Ionic Order in which the volutes are splayed outwards at 45 degrees.
A rounded or convex moulding, such as an ovolo or torus.
A regular structural subdivision or compartment within a building, defined by dividing walls or the positions of the principal supporting members.
A bay in a two storeyed house open to the roof to allow smoke to escape.
An automatic power loom invented by James H Northrop in 1894.
A communal living room.
A capital with an abacus, ovolo, neck and astragal.
A triangular piece of wood used to raise slates next to a wall or similar.

The tower, generally attached to a church or other building but sometimes separate, where bells are hung and from which sound is emitted. Also applied to the upper chamber where bells are hung, or the wooden structure supporting them.
A brace extending from a vertical to a higher horizontal timber.
A principal rafter below a hip rafter that supports the ends of purlins under a hip.
A structural member that supports a purlin and is also a common rafter.
An engine in which a vertical piston rod supplies energy via a crankshaft directly to the flywheel suspended above the cylinder.
A design of vertical steam engine patented in 1802.
A pier used to raise a structure or building above ground floor level so that there is open space beneath the structure
A large room for large numbers of people, eg for balls, meetings etc.
A building, sometimes polygonal in plan, attached to a cathedral, church, abbey etc. and used for the assembly of chapter or brethren, often to address official business.
A doubled rafter, separated by cleats, spacers or distance pieces.
The inner rafter of a compound rafter.
A moulding that projects from a wall above an exterior opening and is shaped to channel rainwater away from it.
A shell-shaped moulded hood over an exterior opening, such as a doorway.
A small structure, often polygonal or circular, on top of a roof, dome, tower etc. featuring apertures to light or ventilate the building it adorns.
A staircase, usually leading from a dormitory, used by monks to enter the transept of a monastic church.

A biggest and plainest of the classical architectural orders, based upon a simplified version of the Doric Order. Sometimes called the Gigantic order, though not to be confused with the Colossal or Giant Order.
A column of the Tuscan Order featuring a plain, unfluted shaft.
An enclosed area, often surrounded by walls or buildings.
A small outer courtyard, or service-yard.
A stone edging on a footway dividing it from a carriageway.
A metal or stone plate or tablet, usually inscribed, set into a wall, pedestal, floor etc., often serving as a memorial.
The principal longitudinal member or spine of a windmill sail, extending from the stock and supporting the sailbars.
The element or elements that make up the different parts of a structural plan.
The main body of a structure not including wings etc.
A block in the position of a tiebeam.
A horizontal member connecting rafters or cruck blades at a point between their feet and the apex of the roof.
A row of columns that carry arches or an entablature.
A series of columns in a straight line supporting an entablature with no openings or glazing.
A plain area situated between the top of the shaft and the capital of a Renaissance Tuscan or Doric Order column.
An opening in the ridge of a roof intended to provide ventilation or an outlet for smoke; also the hood on such an opening that prevents rain entering.
An opening normally in a door or window filled with diagonally set slats to provide ventilation.
A room where organizations and associations meet for the undertaking of presentations and consultation.
A machine for combing out wool, mohair and alpaca, removing short fibres and any extraneous matter, and laying the long fibres more or less parallel to each other.
A device, usually wooden, or fabric on a timber frame, designed to catch, and be driven by, the wind in order to power a windmill. For maritime uses see the Thesaurus of Maritime Archaeology.
A receptacle to hold water used in baptism usually found in a baptistery or nave of a church.
A structure, usually sunk into the ground, for the purpose of allowing total bodily immersion as part of a baptismal ceremony.
An inclined timber running from a rafter to the inner face of a wall, forming a triangle.
Short vertical timbers which go from the rafters to the inner plate on a thick wall.
An entablature of the Tuscan Order consisting of a plain architrave, frieze and cornice without decoration or enrichments..
An order in which the columns rise more than one storey in height from the ground or plinth.
A planked or battened door, with large nails or pegs with projecting heads keeping it intact.
The entrance to a building, apartment or any enclosure consisting of a movable barrier, usually lockable, of wood or other materials.
A joint in which the end is shaped like a dovetail.
A joint in which the dovetailed member is proud of the receiving member and is not finished flush with it.
A stone block set into a wall to support a truss or beam.
A room in which board and table games are usually played.
A room intended for the placement and use of a billiard table.
A decorative ball usually used to ornament a bench end, canopy, gable, pinnacle or spire.
The shaped projection on the top of a newel post or at the apex of a gable.
A main load-bearing horizontal member.
A beam that runs the width of a building.
An oval window placed with its long axis horizontal. The term is often applied to similar round windows.
A slot, usually in a wall, into which a beam was placed.
A principal that has a short, angled section, resembling a knee-joint, at one end.
A structural member that supports a purlin. Use PRINCIPAL RAFTER where the principal is also a common rafter.
A pointed, two-centred, arch whose height is much greater than its span.
A lancet arch designed in the composite style.

A unenclosed feature, usually sub-terranean, constructed to contain water for public or private recreation and sporting activities. Sometimes part of a LIDO complex. Use INDOOR SWIMMING POOL for pools within a covering structure.
Man made structure, usually sub-terranean, built to contain water for the purpose of immersion of people for sport and leisure.
A private bed sitting room.
A machine for combing the fibres of cotton, worsted, flax etc to remove short fibres, clean out extraneous matter, and lay the fibres more or less parallel to each other.
A room within a building or complex, such as a workhouse, used for purifying objects with smoke.
The exercise hall of a Roman baths complex.
A staircase for the use of servants, tradesmen or individuals not permitted to use the main part of a building or establishment.
An arched or vaulted recess, within a tomb or catacomb, intended to contain a coffin or sarcophagus.
A wholly or partially underground chamber, often beneath a chancel, equipped for religious services and usually containing tombs.
A major horizontal timber spanning a roof space to connect a pair of principal rafters and prevent them spreading.
A transverse ceiling beam set below wall-plate level in order to increase head room within the roof space; it carries knee principals, or the struts or braces of an interrupted tiebeam truss.
A four-armed metal coupling, positioned at the centre of a windmill's sails, connected to the front of the striking rod and the shutter bars of all the sails, causing the shutters to open and close.
A room which contains, wholly or partly, a staircase.
An upright framing member, or mullion, that separates two panels, such as in a panelled door or a window.
An addition to the west end of a church which is similar to a porch or narthex but usually consists of a nave and aisles.
An antechurch which is transverse to the axis of the church and is covered or enclosed.
The large main axle on which a waterwheel, which turns the shaft, and a pitwheel are mounted.
A dedicated room for housing the organ feeder bellows, can be an underground chamber or a free-standing building.
A specialised form of crane, usually employed on a dockside, featuring a curving, solid jib.
A crane consisting of a vertical tower or similar structure from which projects a movable boom, or jib, fitted with a mechanical hoist.
A temporary support placed under a vault or arch to support them during construction.
Components within a religious, ritual or funerary site directly connected to the sacred practices involved at the site.
A cupboard set into the eastern wall of a synagogue, facing Jerusalem. The Torah scrolls are kept in the Ark.
A horizontal timber positioned at the bottom of a framed wall into which posts and studs are tenoned.

Series of rooms in a workhouse, prison or hospital, often including porter's lodge, baths, clothes store, washrooms, casual cells, rooms etc, for receiving inmates/patients. Use term in Monument Type Thesaurus if it is a freestanding building.
A assembly room or rooms in which a collection of nominated persons meet to transact business of relevant purpose.
Decorative ornamentation consisting of alternating bead shapes with small, elliptical forms resembling reels
A moulding which is small, convex and circular.
A room in which servants slept.
A mobile crane usually found on a marine or inland quay.
A mobile crane.
A joint in which one timber is reduced in thickness.
A series of concentric mouldings decorating the curved face of an arch.
The ridge formed at the intersection of two vaulted surfaces.
A crane using hydraulic power.
A box crane which uses hydraulic power

An artifically constructed wall with recessed and projecting grips for hands and feet and places to attach ropes. Used for practising rock climbing. If the wall is a free-standing external structure use term for the Thesaurus of Monument Types.
A gate or valve that controls the flow of water from a reservoir, harbour, etc..
A machine for hoisting or moving heavy weights, consisting of a spar or boom set up obliquely, with its head steadied by guys, and furnished with suitable tackle and purchases.
A raised structure from which a priest can preach.
A diagonally set modillion placed at a cornice's external corner.
A small moulded bracket found on the Corinthian and Composite orders, and in plain form in the Ionic.
A small support designed to help support a weight, such as that of a projecting member.
A section of stone or brickwork employed in a timber-framed building to fill the spaces between uprights or studs.
A rib which spans a vault passing from springer to springer at right angles to the axis of the vault.
The top of a cornice in an entablature.
A short principal rafter that that rises from a tiebeam to a collar.
A roof truss supported by arcade posts in an aisled building.
A door adorned with panels, usually arranged in pairs.
An upright member supporting a coping or handrail in a balustrade or staircase.
The structure consisting of a series of balusters supporting a rail or coping, and sometimes a base and pedestal, on a parapet, balcony or staircase.
A waterwheel turned by the weight of water discharged into buckets placed around its circumference from an outlet positioned above it.
An arch where vertical piers raise the springing line.
The horizontal surface of a step or stair.
A pump for removing condensed steam from the condenser of a steam raising plant.
The core of a capital.
An opening or shrine, usually containing a statue, framed by a pair of pilasters or columns supporting an entablature and pediment.
A fereter or repository for relics or any building containing such a repository, or any place or structure where worship is offered or devotions are paid to a saint or deity.
A room in which disused items, such as furniture, are stored.
A rail or balustrade that separates the altar from the rest of the church.
A short timber set on top of two cruck blades.
The large wooden framework that supports the grinding wheels in a corn mill.
A recess or cupboard within a church, usually positioned near the altar, used for the storage of sacred vessels.
A recess, often containing shelves and usually fitted with a door or doors, used for the storage of items.
A room or cupboard usually used for the storage of garments.
A steam engine in which steam flows in one direction only at each stroke, as opposed to the commoner contra-flow engines.
A steam engine in which the cylinder, crosshead and crankshaft lie in a horizontal line, as opposed to vertical designs.
A pair of principal rafters with a tiebeam, usually with a collar.
A convex, finger-like moulding, usually applied in a series to form a pattern of ridges.
A triangular gable usually above an entablature.
A half gable or half pediment concealing the lean-to roof of an aisle.
A movable barrier, usually external, within an opening in a wall, fence, hedge or similar.

A large, movable barrier, usually an iron or wooden grating with pointed bars along its bottom edge, with its sides set in vertical grooves above a castle's gateway enabling it to be dropped to bar entry.
A room or space with an arched ceiling or roof, the depth of which exceeds its span.
A term used to describe a vaulted roof where the vault goes from either the newel of a staircase to a wall or from one wall to another where the walls are circular in plan.
A vault with a ceiling or undersurface subdivided by ribs into bays or webs.
A vault created by the intersection of two other vaults set at right angles to each other and of equal size.
A semi-circular or pointed vault uninterrupted by cross vaults, the ceiling bearing a resemblence to a tunnel or the inside of a barrel.
A triangular or wedge-shaped timber cocking-piece attached to the upper face of a rafter near its foot in order to reduce the slope above the eaves. Such eaves are referred to as being sprocketed.
A window where the sash is hung vertically and the window opens outwards or inwards.
A casement window, carried down to floor level, and opening like two-leafed glazed doors to a garden, veranda or terrace.
A purlin that rests against the lower surface of a principal rafter and is supported by a queen post, collar or raking strut.
A propulsion system for ships using a combination of steam turbines and gas turbines to power the shafts.
A steam motor in which rotory motion is produced by steam impinging directly upon a series of vanes upon the circumference of a revolving cylinder or disc.
A decorative S-shaped moulding, usually placed at the top of a cornice, with a convex lower half and a concave upper half.
A window that forms a projection from the body of a building, extending down to ground level, forming a bay in the wall's interior.
A timber set on the back of a cruck blade to carry a purlin.
Man made structure, usually sub-terranean, built to contain water for the purpose of immersion of people for sport and leisure.
Swimming pool, usually part of a larger LIDO or SPORTS CENTRE complex. Medium size pool, between large pool and paddling pool, used primarily for learners.
An arch built into a wall above another arch or lintel to relieve it of some of the weight of the structure above.
A vertical cladding consisting of tiles hung in overlapping rows.
A non-structural covering, such as tile, slate, wood etc., applied to an exterior wall.
In Orthodox churches, the screen that divides off the sanctuary, usually decorated with sacred images (icons). (Source: Pevsner Glossary)
A large communal room within a college complex used for functions, assemblies, examinations etc.
A large room within a school or college used and appropriately furnished for the sitting of examinations by students..
The principal room in early houses, which occupies the entire height of the building.
A canopy suspended, or on pillars, over a throne or altar.
A window with three openings, the central light being arched and wider than those flanking it.
A horizontal member cutting through the corner formed by two walls to act as a support to a hip rafter to support jetties on two adjacent sides of an upper floor.
A moulding which projects beyond the face of a panel or frame, usually found in panelling or in doors.
A vault where the bay is divided by diagonal and transverse ribs into four cells or webs.
A room for the storage of food and drink.
A screen that separates a tomb or a chapel from the main body of a church.
A window that is lower than others in the chancel side wall.
A room or group of rooms for healing the sick. Where it is a monument use the Thesaurus of Monument Types.
A wall cladding consisting of slates hung in overlapping rows.
A projecting wall or structure, such as a parapet or battlement, supported by a series of corbels.
The heat-resistant wall, or sometimes decorative cast iron panel, at the back of a fireplace.

A large communal room often occupying the full height of the building, used for functions such as meetings, entertainments etc. Traditionally found in medieval buildings but also found in later buildings emulating medieval architecture.
A decorative moulding featuring a repeating star-like motif carved in relief.
A room where letters and parcels are sorted prior to dispatch
An order composed of elements of the Ionic and Corinthian orders.
The horizontal assembly of architrave, frieze and cornice carried upon columns and pilasters in the Composite order; and so containing elements of the Ionic and Corinthian orders.
A series of panels, usually of wood, combined to line a room.
A series of recessed, ornamental panels decorating a ceiling.
A loft, balcony or gallery, in a hall or church, for the use of musicians.
Horizontal timbers laid parallel to each other on which flooring is laid and to which a ceiling is fixed.
A method of floor construction in which sections of joists run at right-angles to one another.
A projecting vertical window in the sloping roof of a house.
A short bay, usually in a hall, commonly as a smoke bay or for a cross-entry.
A large room with fixed seating, often including audio-visual facilites, in which lectures are given.
A room or hall designed for an audience to listen and view, as in a theatre, concert hall, lecture hall etc..
A buttress attached to a corner of a structure, seeming to be a continuation of one of the walls.
An extension to a building, though sometimes contemporary with it, housed under a lean-to roof.
A passage constructed within the thickness of a wall.
One of a pair of posts, placed symmetrically on a tie beam, supporting a collar purlin or plate.
A small square or lozenge-shaped pane of glass as used in a leaded light.
A room within a church or cathedral in which holy relics were kept.
A vertical iron bar fixed within a window opening to support a leaded light.
A cutting, bank or block positioned against the wall of a trench, fort, parapet etc. to allow riflemen to shoot over the crest of the wall or parapet from a standing position.
A firing step from which standing defenders could fire over the crest of a high parapet.
The area or platform at the top of a staircase.
A protective earthern mound.
An open roofed structure on which climbing plants are grown in order to provide a shaded walkway or seating area.
An enclosed area in front of a structure.
An archaeological component which cuts, overlies or abuts other features such as ditches, pits and building foundations.
An archaeological unit of soil in a horizontal plane which may seal features or be cut through by other features.
This is the top term for the class. This class includes terms with very broad definitions which could be assigned to three or more classes. See UNASSIGNED Class List for narrow terms.
The pit in which human or animal remains are placed for interment,which may be either an inhumation or cremation.
A roof with four sloping sides, rising from four gables, joining at the apex like a pyramid.
A transept in which the two wings that extend beyond the walls of the nave are lower than the nave and the crossing of the church.
A cruck with its feet sitting on a ceiling beam on the first floor.
A decorative feature in the form of small pyramids in a continuous line.
A strut which is at an angle between the principal rafter and tiebeam.
A large vertical stone forming part of a structure. If the structure is roofed, the orthostat will stretch from the floor to the roof as a single slab.
A beam which is half the width of a main beam, usually supporting joists.
A steam engine which produces rotary motion.
The junction of two timbers at right angles which involves chamfers or mouldings meeting on a diagonal line and where the timbers meet on the 45 degree line of the chamfer or moulding junction.
A room in which domestic appliances and facilities for cleaning are stored.
The post at the centre of a winding staircase from which the steps radiate; or the post, supporting the handrail and string, at the angle of a square staircase.
A stone or brick structure that projects into, or out of, a room and contains the flue.
The flue or channel that funnels smoke from a fire.
An area of plasterwork modelled or incised with ornamental designs or motifs.
Ornamental plasterwork applied to the exterior of a building, especially timber-framed houses of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries..
The use of knapped flint applied to a wall to form a decorative pattern.
A Capital with a rolled up cushion-like form on either side creating distinctive volutes.
A type of Ionic diagonal capital where all four sides are the same and with therefore eight volutes.
A capital at the corner of a colonade or portico in the Ionic Order in which the volutes are splayed outwards at 45 degrees.
A hydraulic engine patented in 1830 comprising a large specially shaped cast-iron outer casing enclosing a close-fitting but separate solid globe.
An engine, introduced in Germany 1748 for use in mines, worked by high pressure water.
A funnel, connected to an opening in the floor of the tun encasing the grindstones in a corn mill, that carries ground meal away to a bin or sack.
A sitting room fitted with large windows for the purpose of admitting the maximum amount of sunlight.
Originally a room in a house where a family would gather, usually in the evening, to sit and read, write, sew, play music etc. This term has become commonly used as an alternative to living room.
A detached external pier by which an arch or vault is prevented from spreading.
The surface of a room which conceals overhead structural members.
A ceiling that is curved or vaulted, sometimes employed to give a space the appearance of a vault.
An arch, either pointed or round, which broadens above the springing line, creating a horseshoe shape.
A stone screen, usually found in a monastic or collegiate church, that separates the choir from the nave.
A stand, either fixed or free-standing, usually with a sloping surface from which a bible, book or notes can be read, found in a church or lecture theatre.
Small ornamental pyramid or cone, often decorated, crowning a spire, turret, buttress etc.
A hall, or main living-room in a medieval house, open to the roof.

An arrangement by which panels, screens, vaults or windows are divided into parts of different shapes or sizes by means of moulded stone bars or ribs, called form-pieces or forms in the Medieval period.
An ornamental foliation in panels or tracery so called because it is arranged around five intervals, known as foils or cusps, that describe the outlines of five leaves or petals.
The first driven gearwheel on the vertical shaft within a corn mill which meshes with the brake wheel in a windmill, or the pit wheel in a watermill.
A projection left on a stone block to allow it to be hoisted into position.
A low bank on the edge of a defensive ditch on the side away from the main rampart.
The lower, horizontal surface of a room on which people walk. Can also mean a storey of a building.
A surface made up of small pieces of hard material such as stone or tile, often in a decorative pattern.
The consecrated ground immediately surrounding a tomb or grave.
A rib placed at an angle to support timbers.
A quadrangle or courtyard, often grassed or planted, surrounded by cloisters
A sculptural or decorative representation of an animal. Includes mythical and fantastical animals such as dragons, centaurs etc.

A classical ornamentation featuring foliage, fruits and, unlike Arabesque, human and animal figures, sometimes fantastic or mythological in nature. The term may have originated from the fact that many examples of such ornamentation were found in grottoes.
A chimney placed at an angle with the walls so that there is an obtuse angle with each wall.
A joint with each timber ending in a cut slanted across its length.
A decorative feature of a shield for armorial bearings.
A display or depiction of authorised armorial bearings, such as the elements of a coat of arms, livery and personal badges and flags, mottoes etc..
A wooden panel, mounted on the wall of a church, bearing the Achievement of Arms of an individual whose funeral has taken place there.
A room in a house, hotel, business premises etc. where guests or visitors are received.
A swimming pool restricted to the use of females only.
A truss in which two pairs of arch braces form a continuous curve from the wall-heads or below to meet in the centre of the collar.
A mechanism, either automated or consisting of winches and pulleys, that raises sacks of grain on ropes or chains from one floor of a mill to another, usually through one or a series of trap doors.
A chamber built into a wall in a defensive structure.
A small timber fixed to a wall to support items such as cladding. Also a strip of timber fixed across two or more parallel boards to join them together or conceal their join.
The principal storey of a building, containing the main reception rooms, when not situated on ground level.
A wing joined to the main body of the building with the roof at right angles to the main roof.
The non-diagonal joint at the right-angled meeting of two stone mouldings.

One of two or three bands on a classical architrave, each projecting slightly beyiond the one below, often separated by enriched mouldings. Also a vertical band or board, such as that over a shop front on which its name is painted.
A stud on the outside of a cruck blade and attached to it.
A subsidiary member, usually vertical, in a framed wall or partition.
A form of tandem compounding of early beam engines, with a high pressure steam cylinder mounted on top of a low pressure one, introduced in Cornwall the 1840s by James Sims.
A room set aside within a building where social and other functions such as parties, presentations and meetings can be held.
A flat-topped, often elongated, construction at which customers are served by staff in a commercial establishment such as a bank, shop, inn etc.
A counter from which beverages are served in a bar room.
A capital featuring stylised, often projecting, three-lobed leaves.
An inward radial-flow reaction turbine developed by James Thomson.

A prime mover in which a central wheel with curved vanes, enclosed in a chamber, receives water directed onto the curved vanes by a ring of fixed guide vanes, causing the wheel to rotate about its axis.
A framed fireplace hood built on a bressumer and extending from one outer wall to the other or to a spere.
An open roofed structure on which climbing plants are grown in order to provide a shaded walkway or seating area.
A vertical piece of wood fixed on corners of walls to help the fixing of plaster and to protect against damage to the plaster.
An ornamental moulding consisting of a series of small, regularly placed squared or rounded blocks.
A series of billet mouldings forming a decorative band of small, regularly placed squared or rounded blocks.
A structure, part of a structure or area constructed from plain bricks.
An aisle divided into chapels. This term is sometimes applied to the screens dividing the aisle, or the chapels themselves.

The side compartment of a building, usually a church, hall or barn, separated from the main body of the building by an arcade; or a passage allowing access to rows of seats in an auditorium or church.
A narrow aisle that pierces an internal buttress.
An aisle within a chancel providing access to, and around, the High Altar and surrounding cells.
An inscription on illustration applied to a wall by carving into its plaster to reveal a different colour beneath.
A vertical structure usually with an Attic base or spira with slender and elegant design, with a capital decorated with acanthus leaves and an entablature rich in carved ornament.
An area or wing of a hexagonal plan workhouse or prison from where the supervision of the inmates is carried out.
An enclosure or niche in a library or cloister used for study.
A shallow, ornamental recess, often arched and semicircular in plan, set in a wall or pier and usually containing a statue or ornament.
A wooden bench with a back and ends, usually fixed and found in churches. The bench ends are often decorated.
An enclosed, high-sided seat, sometimes entered through a door, usually found in churches as one of a series of box-like compartments.
A three-dimensional decorative ornamentation composed of a series of Vs forming a zigzag.
A tall structure, high in proportion to its lateral dimensions, often rising in stages, free standing or part of another building, surmounted by a clock.
A bracket that is shaped to form an angle.
One of a pair of crossing braces halved or lapped across each other, tying a pair of rafters together.
An arch with jambs that do not stand at an angle of ninety degrees to its face.
A pavilion ward in a workhouse, used to accommodate inmates
Fine art depiction in oil, watercolour or other media on wood panels, canvas or paper
A hall normally reserved for holding feasts on special occasions.
A gulley in the form of a circle for holding vertical posts in the walls of iron age round houses.
Room within a building or complex used by a porter.
A straight brace from rafter to collar in a common rafter truss.
A V-shaped gutter.
A trough or watercourse constructed to collect rainwater from a roof and channel it towards a drain or pipe.
A roof consisting of a series of rafters and arch braces set closely together to give the appearance of a continuous, uninterrupted arch or curved plate.
A joint consisting of a projecting tongue fitting into an open housing in a scarf joint or the apex of a rafter.
A channel or pipeline that feeds water to a waterwheel.
An interior or projecting shelter surrounding an entrance to a building.
A porch which is sufficiently large enough to allow a vehicle beneath it.
A porch with columns and pediments.
A decorative moulding shaped to resemble a length of cable or twisted rope.
A half or whole baluster applied to a wall or pedestal, thus described as engaged.
A store for undressed meat, usually for keeping grouse, partridge, pheasant or rabbit in a cool environment.
A room in which food, usually meat, is kept.
A cruck blade, sometimes jointed, set in the centre of a gable wall, usually of a cruck-framed building.
A room for storing and maintaining the equipment used in riding, eg saddles, bridles, reins etc.
A room or group of rooms usually reserved for use by the monarchy or government.
A room for higher-ranking servants.
A gutter running behind a parapet wall, the water channelled by it being discharged from holes in the wall.
An elevated table or podium on which to place, or sacrifice, offerings to a god or gods.
A room usually set aside for the provision of materials used in a household.
A purlin that is joined to the back of a principal rafter.
A rafter of uniform size placed at regular intervals along the length of a roof.
The rotating top section of a tower or smock mill.
A horizontal, stationary steam engine with two cylinders in line, one behind the other, the steam passing through each cylinder in turn.
A tower which rises above the space at the intersection of the nave, chancel and transepts of a church. Usually pierced by windows to provide light directly to the area below.
A sloping timber in a staircase holding the ends of treads and risers.
A compact steam engine of moderate power in which the steam cylinder was placed vertically on a table with the crosshead and guides mounted immediately above.
A room dedicated to the drawing or letting of blood, a procedure once thought to possess medicinal properties.
A covered walkway attached to a building.
An area of several rooms in a medieval house or building. Probably for domestic use.
An entablature with a plain architrave or principal beam over which are the frieze and cornice.
A solid support of masonry to sustain vertical pressure and frequently each of the pillars from which an arch springs.
A pier which supports a gate.
An inclined course on top of a projecting sill, cornice etc., designed to throw off rainwater.
A representation of the crucifixion of Christ or related scenes as a sculpture, etc.
A figurative or abstract design in the round or in relief and can be of various materials. For freestanding sculptures see also Monument Types Thesaurus.
A carved stone female figure often found on castles and churches.
A window divided into lights by mullions.
A semi-circular opening or window divided, by two mullions, into three sections, the central section being wider than those either side of it.
A shelf that is behind an altar.

A decorative, repeating geometric pattern, utilizing motifs such as lozenges, leaves etc., applied to a wall or panel, or incorporated into brickwork by laying different coloured bricks in a series of squares, diamonds.
A gap which allows air to circulate.
A building forming part of a complex or larger establishment, such as a workhouse, that serves as an entrance equipped with facilities associated with procedures undergone by new arrivals.
A machine for spinning cotton yarn.
A flat, horizontal piece of timber or masonry projecting from or recessed into a wall to hold objects.
A rafter set outside a gable to carry the roof beyond the wall face.
A small room off a bedroom, usually in eighteenth century houses, in which a person would have their wig powdered.
