An enclosed structure, usually glass fronted, designed to contain, and facilitate the viewing of, objects or notices. May be built into the fabric of a room, affixed to an exterior or exterior wall or free standing.

A steam engine in which the spent steam leaving the cylinder passes to a condenser instead of being exhausted to the atmosphere. The condenser creates a partial vacuum which increases the pressure drop across the engine, so raising its efficiency.
The most common form of joint between two timbers meeting at right angles or at an oblique angle, the mortice being a socket cut in one timber to receive the tenon projection of the other.
A piece of machinery for lifting stone, or containers loaded with stone, from a quarry.
A simple type of hinge used for large gates.
A movable joint, usually consisting of two plates joined by a pin, that fastens a door, gate, window etc. to its frame and allows it to open and shut.
A strut which is part of a pair which support the collar and are supported by the tiebeam.
A small semicircular moulding.
A principal that has been reduced to the same thickness as a common rafter where the roof has clasped purlins.
A projection from the inner angle of a building around a court or with advancing wings, usually containing a staircase and often crowned above the roof line with an ogee dome.
Man made structure to contain tidal waters, often part of a LIDO, bathing or swimming complex at the sea edge.
A first-floor chamber, often serving as a parlour, bedchamber or private room, in a medieval house. Also a loft, rood-loft or garret.
A gutter running behind a parapet wall, the water channelled by it being discharged from holes in the wall.
A barrier, usually in the form of a small wall, set to prevent people falling over the side of a bridge, balcony, roof etc.
An indented parapet consisting of raised parts, called merlons, separated by gaps, or crenelles.
Originally, the inner sanctuary of a Greek temple from where the oracle was delivered. Now used for any private chamber or sanctuary.
A circular window with tracery or shafts radiating from the centre.
A pointed arch in which the arcs comprising it are centred on points both outside and inside the arch so that the point takes the form of a double S, or ogee.
An arch produced by two curves, usually each with a radius equal to the span, which meet at a point.
A small hole filled or vacated by a stake, normally driven into the ground.
A feature found during an archaeological investigation which implies the presence of a monument, building, structure or human activity.
A joint which has a splay with a short perpendicular section at each end.
A piece of stone or timber with a small cup shaped depression in its upper surface, set into the ground to fasten a door, gate, window etc to allow it to open and shut.
An ornamental bracket on either side of a doorway supporting a cornice.
A plain stone course, sometimes projecting, above a cornice and counterbalancing the cantilevered sections of stone, or at the base of a building.
A room set aside for the purpose of reading, such as in a library or a club.
A mechanism within a windmill, attached to the striking rod, allowing the opening and closing of the shutters or vanes of patent sails.
A diagonal or arched member placed across rafters to stiffen and support a roof structure.
A horizontal member along the length of an aisle wall to receive the ends of rafters.
A small auxiliary engine to help start up a large steam engine - invented 1866.
The lowest part, or base, of a structure, sometimes built into the ground, on which the weight of the whole rests.
The quasi-triangular area between, and defined by, the upper sections of a pair of adjacent arches, or between the curve of an arch and the angle of a surrounding frame or structure.
A spandrel that is sunk below the surface of the arch.
A spandrel which supports a dome and aids the transition between the dome and its square or octagonal base.
A detached, sometimes monolithic vertical structural element usually circular, square or polygonal bearing an ornate eight-voluted Ionic Capital.
A piece of wood of square or rectangular section driven through a scarf joint to tighten it
A propulsion system for ships using a combination of steam turbines and gas turbines to power the shafts.
A heat engine prime mover in which hot gases or hot air expand as they pass through and rotate vanes attached to a central shaft, enclosed in a chamber.

A propulsion system for ships which need a maximum speed that is considerably faster than their cruise speed, particularly warships. It consists of diesel engines for cruising and gas turbines for high-speed transits.
A spirally turned baluster.
One of a pair of inclined struts that meet at the bottom to form a V shape.
A covering of metal, stone or pottery set on a chimney breast above a fire to prevent smoke entering the room and instead directing it up the chimney.
Circular or almond-shaped halo surrounding the figure of Christ, the Virgin or a saint.
An arch associated with the portion of the stage between the curtain and the orchestra.
A very high chimney, though more often a chimney stack containing only one flue.
A cube-shaped capital with its lower corners rounded to facilitate the transition from the circular shaft below to the square abacus it supports.
A type of cushion capital with the lunette on each face cut into a series of truncated cones to form a scallop shell-like decoration.
The framing of a door or window with two columns piers or pilasters which support a lintel,entablature etc.
Series of rooms within a workhouse where elderly men were housed.
A mobile crane usually found on a marine or inland quay.
A crane usually found at a marine or inland quay.
A passage providing access to utility services or a larger tunnel for the purposes of monitoring and maintenance. May also provide be used as an evacuation route.
A semi-automatic loom developed in Lancashire. Although it is self-acting, it has to be stopped to recharge empty shuttles.
A small moulding on the top of the architrave in the Doric order.

Any structural component, usually of wood, which is believed to have derived from a vessel. Double index with the appropriate type of structural component eg. BEAM or if known the original term from the MARITIME FIXTURES AND FITTINGS thesaurus.

An entablature often of great magnificence with bead and reel between fasciae of the architrave, frieze ornamented with continuous sculpture and cornice, with ornate coffers and richly carved modillions.
A quoin, usually of stone, with a concave rebate at one corner forming the surface against which the rounded edge of a canal lock gate turns, making a kind of hinge.
A stone or brick used on a corner to reinforce it and also as decoration.
A channel, usually placed upstream of a mill, that carries water from a river or other water source to a waterwheel.
A crane used for moving coal during the refuelling of coal-powered vessels or locomotives.
A decorative feature, or non-heraldic badge, representing the name of a person connected with a building, often in the form of a graphic pun on the name.
A glazed opening in a roof or ceiling.
An arch at the liturgical east of the nave, carrying a gabled wall above, separating nave from chancel.
Decorative ornamentation in the form of interlacing ribbons or cords, usually in an intricate pattern.
A timber running obliquely across a series of common rafters.
A column base featuring a hollow, concave - or scotia - moulding between two tori, which are projecting, convex mouldings.
A semicircular niche or recess in a wall.
A room set aside within a club, hotel, large residence etc. for the benefit of guests or individuals who wish to smoke.
A decorative moulding in the form of a chain.
An Italian cornice consisting of a bed moulding, a row of plain block corbels or modillions, and a corona or cornice.
A room used in a church to store vestments and for the clergy to dress in.
An outward projection from a defensive wall allowing the defenders to attack the flanks of any force attacking the wall.
A covered ambulatory, usually arcaded, arranged around three or four sides of a quadrangle in a monastic or collegiate establishment.
A path or area for exercise, usually taken in the form of walking. Can be partially or totally covered.
Pinion which engages with the spur wheel and drives the millstones in a corn mill.
An iron bar or rod running through the core of a windmill's windshaft that causes the opening and shutting of the vanes on patent sails.
The bottom storey of a building, often below or partially below ground level.
A continuous series of ornaments within a horizontal moulding.
A decorative ornamentation of interlaced bands in the form of a plait.
An entablature consiting of an architrave, usually a frieze and cornice.
A short timber connecting a cruck's timber, or blade, to a wall plate.
One of a series of small, rectangular blocks arranged like a row of teeth, projecting from the lower part of a cornice in many classical orders.
A compact, free standing steam engine introduced circa 1810 and built mainly in small sizes.
A windbrace that bypasses one purlin to be jointed into the next purlin or principal.
Solid masonry placed to counteract the lateral thrust of a vault or arch.
A place set apart to contain books for reading, study or reference.
A complete cross-frame from ground level to ridge in a box-framed building.
The main gear wheel, which is engaged by a brake to halt or slow the machinery, mounted on the windshaft of a windmill.
A type of foundation in the form of a post sunk into the ground to support a building platform.
Decorative ornamentation consisting of alternating oval and V-shaped motifs, used to enrich ovolo mouldings.
An exterior, vertical pipe that carries rainwater from a roof's guttering, usually to a drain.
A hollow, cylindrical structure or tube made of clay, lead, steel, wood etc. through which gas, oil, water or other liquids can be conveyed.
Small 'drops' or conic projections under the mutules (a repeating block motif) and triglyphs (rectangular blocks embellished with vertical grooves) in a Doric entablature.
A tower, normally forming part of a larger structure, housing a staircase.
An octagonal spire atop a square tower, the transition between structures effected by partially pyramidical masonry forms called broaches.
An improved water frame spinning machine which differed from the water frame in mechanical details, being larger and operating at a much faster speed.
A machine used for spinning cotton powered by water.
A room in a public house, hotel, club etc. containing a bar from which alcoholic drinks are served.
The main vertical axle running through the centre of a mill.
A power driven cloth-weaving machine.
A room for holding receptions particularly balls.
A crane operated by a treadmill turned by the walking action of men placed within it.
A wedge shaped stone or brick forming part of an arch or vault, with its radiating sides coinciding with the radii of that arch.
One of a pair of straight or curved members set at an angle on the tie-beam and framed into a principal rafter, often supporting a purlin.
A fixed seat, often enclosed, usually found within an ecclesiastical building and frequently arranged in series to accommodate a congregation, choir or clergy.
A horizontal, supporting beam set above an opening in an external wall, a fireplace opening or in timber framed construction set forward from the lower part of a building to support a jettied wall.
A branched, hanging light fitting, often ornate in design, provifding illumination from a number of candles or lightbulbs.
A decorative form consisting of two upright S-shaped curves positioned opposite one another with their concave sections placed at the top.
A Saxon quoin consisting of alternating horizontally and vertically laid stones.
A concave moulding where the profile is a quarter of a circle.
A room in a monastery used by monks or nuns as a day room.
A curb-roof with curved or steeply pitched lower slopes and a hipped or pitched roof above. Named after the French Classical architect F. Mansart.
A pair of common rafters with a collar.
A room for washing and cleaning linen.
A room that is underground or partially underground. Primarily used for storage.
A large, high-ceilinged room, usually in a great house or palace, serving as a principal reception room; also a room used by the public for a specific purpose; or a drawing room in a house.

A capital with two rows of eight acanthus leaves rising above an astragal;from between the leaves of the upper row rise eight stalks,each surmounted by a calyx from which emerge volutes carryingthe corners of the concave sided abacus and central ornaments
A section of a window between mullions; thus for example a three-light window.
A wooden bar sliding from a long channel at one side of a doorway into a socket in the other jamb so as to bar the door.
An enclosed, defensible passageway, equipped with gun ports, extending into or traversing a dry ditch.
A room for children to play in.
A joint in which both timbers are reduced in thickness.
A ceiling beam aligned with the length of a building.
A truss which does not subdivide a space, as in the centre of a hall of two bays or in an undivided building such as a barn.
An outward radial flow reaction water turbine.
A mixed-flow reaction water turbine, developed in the USA in 1840 by James Bichino Francis, in which water enters the runner radially and leaves axially.
A stone, usually inscribed, laid during a ceremony to commemorate the founding of a new building
A decorative feature, usually Norman, in which fluting takes a spiral rather than vertical form.
The point or block from which an arch or vault starts. The block generally has a horizontal base and sloped top.
The cold room in a Roman baths.
A decorative moulding depicting an animal or bird head, similar to a beak head moulding but with a shorter, cone-like tongue or beak overlapping the moulding set beneath it.
A usually shallow cut linear feature often used for drainage.
The application of colour and other materials to a structure to either conceal it from identification or to make it look like something else.
A room, usually at ground level or below, housing the tank in which water is heated as part of a building's heating system.
A crane deriving its power from steam generated from solid fuels.
A room used for storing food.
A large tank or cask for storing or holding goods, whether liquid, such as chemicals or fermenting beer, or dry, often during a production process.
A grave or sepulchre enlosed or covered by a memorial.
A room in which commercial or administrative activities are carried out.
An aperture cut obliquely in a wall, usually of a chancel, affording a view between the high altar and the aisles or side chapels.
A panel, usually wooden or stone, painted or engraved with an illustration or inscription.
A defined portion of a surface, often a wall lining or door, usually rectangular, which may be recessed, level with or proud of its surrounding surface and may be framed by mouldings or channels.
A decorative moulding featuring a geometric pattern suggesting the crenelles and merlons of a battlement.
A slope, usually with rails, by which a boat can be transferred from one level of waterway to another.
A capital with a half figure projecting from each corner
A decorative style on a block or band from which an arch springs.
The band or block from which the springing of an arch occurs.
A steam engine in which the pistons are long relative to their diameters, and there are no piston rods and crossheads.
A brace, running from a post to a principal, stiffening an interrupted tiebeam.
A post, usually carrying a tiebeam, which forms part of a wall in a timber framed building.
A framework of crossed or parallel wooden or metal bars placed over an opening to prevent access but allow the passage of air, light, water etc.
A joint in which the side, or face, of a timber has been squarely cut away to fit into a corresponding cut-away in the side of another member.
A compact design of engine in which a connecting rod is dispensed with, giving a short baseplate.
A beam that has been applied to a wall face.
The wedge shaped end of a bridge pier designed to break the current of water.
A metallic rod attached to a high point on a building and leading to the earth for the purpose of conducting lightning away from the main body of the building.
A circular window containing tracery.
An upright post set on a tiebeam or collar and used to support a ridge piece.
A column that is equidistant, with intercolumniations, even at the corners, with two or more triglyphs over each intercolumniation.
The top stone in a gable end.
The transverse portion of a cruciform church, or the arms on either side of the crossing, often with chapels at the east sides and possibly with aisles as well.
The first court which is before the principal court.
A raised platform, especially in a theatre or auditorium, for the use of performers or speakers etc.
A dedicated room for housing the organ feeder bellows, can be an underground chamber or a free-standing building.
A projecting structure in the roof of a mill containing a winch, allowing loads to be lifted clear of the building's wall and protecting the winch from the weather.
A joint in which a dovetailed timber's surface is proud of the receiving member on one side.

A loom developed by Joseph Jacquard in 1801 which used a series of punched cards to control the movement of the warp threads thereby allowing complex patterns to be created without the intervention of the weaver
A wheeled device supporting a windmill's fantail that moves around a trackway thus turning the windmill into the wind.
A steeple in which the spire resembles a crown.
A collective term embracing a church tower's entire structure, including its spire.

The common design of steam engine, where steam enters the cylinder at one end, and after it has pushed the piston to the other end, its direction of flow is reversed as the piston returns, the steam leaving the cylinder through the exhaust ports.
A sitting room or lounge in a house intended mainly for use during the morning.
A room for general daily use, usually in a family dwelling.
A decorative moulding depicting an animal or bird head with a tongue or beak that overlaps the moulding set below it.
A flight of stairs, situated on a building's exterior, extending to an upper storey.
A small tank or cistern, often lead or cast-iron and sometimes ornamented, that collects rainwater from a gutter or channel prior to it being discharged.
A private chamber usually in a Royal residence.
A small piece of timber occupying the angle between two other timbers.
A buttress set away from the structure it supports, joined to it by an arch.
A joint with a loose tenon morticed into two touching pieces of timber

A hole in a wall in which cross timbers, known as putlogs, were placed to allow scaffolding to be erected. Putlog holes are often left unfilled and are often the only evidence for the use of scaffolding.
Any member that connects two bodies, such as the sloping sides of a roof, to prevent them pulling apart.
A room where fittings such as saddles and harnesses for horses are usually kept.
A large concave moulding.
An inner defensive area within a castle.
The horizontal assembly of architrave, frieze and cornice carried upon columns and pilasters in the Composite order; and so containing elements of the Ionic and Corinthian orders.
A sculpted female figure carrying a basket on her head.
A half-pier, corbel or other architectural element, usually situated at the end of an arcade, attached to a wall to support an arch.
A single acting, non-rotative beam engine worked by atmospheric pressure.

A large shallow pan, usually metal, in which seawater or brine is evaporated for the extraction of salt. The name is also often applied to the exterior pits or ponds used for the same purpose (see Thesaurus of Monument Types).
A joint in which the end of one timber is cut in V- form and rests on the edge of another timber, frequently found between common rafters and wall plates.
A strategically placed aperture in the wall of a fortification through which guns and cannon can fire upon an invading force.
An engine in which the oscillating motion of the beam is translated via gearing to a rotative motion.
A rotative beam steam engine patented in 1790.
A small self-contained rotative Beam Steam Engine.
A board fixed at the gable end to purlins, often decorated.
A depressed arch formed around three arcs: two struck from the springing line with one centrally placed arc struck from below it.
An opening in a structure which is arched.
An hydraulically powered machine that tips coal into railway wagons, usually situated at a pithead.
A room where meals, particularly breakfast are partaken.
An open grating of wrought iron, bronze, wood etc., forming a screen to a door, window or other opening, or used as a divider.
The horizontal timber running the length of a roof and positioned at its apex.
A truss in which spaces between timbers are filled, as between rooms and at the ends of a building.
A base on which pews or benches can be placed.
A metal safe built in to wall
A thin, rectangular panel, sometimes enriched with sculptural relief, placed between the triglyphs in a Doric Order frieze.
A small room communicating with one or more apartments.
A capital of the Tuscan Order featuring a square abacus.
The post at the centre of a winding staircase from which the steps radiate; or the post, supporting the handrail and string, at the angle of a square staircase.
A hidden dungeon entered through a trap door from the room above it. Also a similarly concealed pit into which prisoners would be cast.
A chamber for the incarceration of one or more prisoners.
A recess or chamber near the altar which holds a relic. A feature of medieval churches.
The commonest type of roof with sloping sides meeting at a ridge and with a gable at each end.
A pole projecting to the rear of a post mill to allow the sails to be moved into the wind.
Romanesque overlapping arcades, producing a series of pointed arches.
A light, usually encased and mounted upon brackets, often used outdoors. It may be powered by gas, electricity or house a candle.
The gearwheel on the upright shaft within a windmill from which secondary drives operating other parts of the machinery are powered. are taken
A room, or part of a kitchen, for the cleaning and storage of kitchen utensils.
A purlin that is threaded through a hole in the principal rafter.
A short beam that projects into a roof space from the foot of a principal rafter to receive a hammer post and, often, an arched brace.

A section of a building distinguished from the rest either architecturally or as a separate structure joined to the main building, often intended for entertainment, specialised functions or ornamental purposes.
A latrine built into, or projecting beyond, the wall of a medieval castle featuring a stone or wooden seat situated above a pit or the castle moat.
A wall-mounted moulding defining the upper edge of the dado, intended to protect the wall's surface from damage caused by chairs being pushed against it.
A room decorated, and furnished, in the style of the Chinese.
A cylindrical, polygonal, or square element of brick, metal or terracotta fixed on top of a chimney stack to extend the flue and improve the extraction of smoke.
A prime mover which is rotated by the reaction from forcing a moving fluid to change direction.
The outer curve of an arch or vault.
A room for temporary imprisonment and detention at a Police Station.
A crane that is fixed to a quay and used for the loading and unloading of cargo.
A late 12th Century capital featuring large, unribbed leaves growing outwards from each angle before curving up towards the abacus and turning inwards at the top.
Man made shallow pool, usually made for children to play in rather than swim.
A horizontal, moulded band, usually projecting, running across a wall, often indicating the position of a building's interior floor.
A short timber set diagonally in the spandrel of an arch brace, between the brace and the corner of the main frame.
A strut with a reflecting counterpart that forms the letter A on either side of a king post.
A semicircular niche in the wall of a mosque used to indicate the Qiblah - the direction to be faced during prayers. Qiblah is set as the direction of the Kaaba in Mecca.
A window in the form of a three-leafed clover.
An aisle divided into chapels. This term is sometimes applied to the screens dividing the aisle, or the chapels themselves.

The side compartment of a building, usually a church, hall or barn, separated from the main body of the building by an arcade; or a passage allowing access to rows of seats in an auditorium or church.
A mobile crane which floats on water.
A flat, horizontal piece of timber or masonry projecting from or recessed into a wall to hold objects.

A panel, board or device bearing an inscription, symbol or image, usually describing a function, name, location, instruction, or otherwise distinguishing the object or structure on which it is placed.
The impost, or point at which a vault rests upon its support, and from which it seems to spring. Hence the term describes the bottom stone of a vault, which lies on the impost.
A timber frame placed in the bell chamber of a church tower or belfry designed to hold the bells.
A booth, cubicle or recess, often enclosed, in which a priest, usually screened from view, hears confessions.
A suite of rooms.
A termination, either in the form of a decorative feature or a right-angle applied to the end of the moulding, of a label, dripstone or string course.
A brace which is cut to a double curve.
A base of the Tuscan Order, featuring a square plinth supporting a large torus with a fillet above.
A room, usually lined with wood panels, that is heated to a hot and steamy temperature using a small stove and intended to be used for relaxation and therapeutic purposes.
A passageway or corridor beteen two areas which is covered.
A secondary rib usually springing from the junction of two other ribs, or one that rises between a main diagonal and transverse rib, from the springing to the ridge rib.
A moulding on the neck of a column.
A room in a church where vestments and sacred vessels are stored.
A joint in which the end of one timber is shaped to fit around the moulding on another.
A window cut through a single slab of timber or stone.
A deeply concaved moulding, with a projecting lower edge, that sits at the base of a column between two torus mouldings.
A staircase where the vertical members are placed at the ends of flights to support the strings, handrails, trimmers and bearers.
A horizontal door within, and usually flush with surface of, a floor, ceiling or roof.
A roof, circular in plan, usually semicircular in section, over a circular, square or polygonal base.
A roof truss used for small spans with a saw-tooth overall shape braced by vertical and diagonal members.
An external wall cladding consisting of overlapping horizontal boards.
A place in which brewing and sometimes baking took place.
A tank used for the storage of water, usually found as part of an institution like a workhouse or hospital complex
The hole through which the fuel for a fire is put to start and maintain the fire.
A projecting part of a building that overhangs the wall below it.
The bracket under a jetty bressumer.
A room to withdraw to from the dining room.
A type of light fixture affixed to a wall in such a way that it uses only the wall for support, and the light is usually directed upwards.
A framed canopy suspended above the dais or high table at the upper end of a hall.
A part of a building or complex where administrative duties are undertaken.
A hydraulic machine used to drive a pillar crane.
A roof with a slope of less than ten degrees to the horizontal.
A horizontal member of a wall frame between posts or studs, also the horizontal member in panelling, a panelled door or a window.
A pedestrian passageway, often elevated, connecting two buildings or sections within a building.
A decorative moulding featuring a series of small, projecting, dome-like shapes each resembling a halved nutmeg.
A timber placed horizontally in a cob or stone wall to support the foot of a post or cruck blade.
A room before a larger room which is often used as a waiting room.
A joist which rests on top of a beam.
A ticket booth in a theatre or cinema.
The area between the base and the cornice of a plinth.
A form of pilaster without base or capital.
A flattened rectangular version of a column of one of the classical orders.
A roof constructed of horizontal tiebeams and curved trusses.
A roof with a bow-shaped, segmental timber truss which has the space between its members filled by a lattice of crossing diagonal rods.
A buttress which encases the corner between two walls.
A transverse ceiling beam set below wall-plate level in order to increase head room within the roof space; it carries knee principals, or the struts or braces of an interrupted tiebeam truss.
A room where a committee meets to discuss matters put before them.
An aisle within a chancel providing access to, and around, the High Altar and surrounding cells.
A partition or wall behind an altar, often separating the choir and altar from the ambulatory and east chapel.
A composite beam of timber strengthened by an iron plate, or plates, used in the construction of 18th and 19th century timber framed mills and warehouses.
A projection from the bottom of a church stall designed to provide some support for a person standing in long services.
A semicircular crypt below the apse of a church.

A room provided for the use of people waiting for a service, such as a train or bus service, or to see an individual such as a doctor or a dentist. When a dedicated structure, see Thesaurus of Monument Types.
A concealed passage or room for the purpose of hiding and protecting a (Roman Catholic) priest during times of religious persecution.
A pointed, two centred, arch whose span is greater than its height.
A room in which music is performed.
Decorative ornamentation of a key pattern
An opening through which missiles or combustible materials could be dropped upon attackers. Usually through the roof of an entrance way or in a projecting parapet.
A room where weapons and armour are stored.
Two linked quadrangular open areas surrounded by walls or buildings.
A brace running from a vertical to a lower horizontal timber.

The upper parts of walls carried on arcades or colonnades in the nave, choir or transepts of a church or hall, rising higher than the lean-to roofs of the aisles and pierced with windows to allow light to penetrate.
The main vertical beam standing at the centre of a post mill carrying the bearing upon which the body of the mill rotates.
A steam engine from which the spent exhaust steam either passes out to the atmosphere and is lost, or in some cases is used as process steam in a factory.
A horizontal, riven board, tapered in section, used for external cladding.
A plinth or pedestal, sometimes decorated, and sometimes supporting statuary, placed at the apex and lower extremity of a pediment.
A small apse.
A polygonal or semicircular recess projecting from an external wall, often with a rounded vault.
A container for holding water in which to bathe.
A spire, or spirelet surmounting a roof, especially over the crossing of a French Gothic Cathedral.
A window set within a curved projection from the face of a wall.
The smallest piece of timber (2-5cms) across used in building, employed on rafters to support the roof covering or in a partition as a base for plaster or external render and wall covering.
A staircase divided into two parallel flights joined by a landing with no well between the outer strings.
A private room or parlour usually at the same level as the hall.
In a truss, a rafter used to stiffen the principal rafter by doubling it.
A cornice along the sloping edge of a pediment or gable.
The area at the end of an axle or shaft around which a wheel, bearing etc. pivots or rotates.
A room equipped for the care of babies and young children.
A room within a building or complex used for storing clothing.
A decorated beam, above the dais or high table, attached to the partition at the upper end of a hall.
A case or border, usually wood or metal, enclosing a picture, painting or photograph.
A window in a spire, often in the form of a dormer.
A capital of the Tuscan Order featuring a square abacus.
A carved, convex block, often richly decorated, at the intersections of vault-ribs, beam-junctions or terminations of mouldings.
The skirting or projecting base of a wall.
A structure, usually of brick, stone or timber, that encloses or defines a space or room.
An interior wall, usually not load bearing, that divides a space.
A wall connecting two pavilions, bastions, towers etc..
The lower area of a interior wall between the skirting board and the dado rail
A block in the position of a tiebeam.
A grooved block placed in series, alternating with the metopes, in a Doric frieze.
A joist that has its end resting in a recess in a transverse beam.
A meeting room intended specifically for the use of a board of directors or governors of an organization or company..
A cruck which is set in solid masonry above floor level but stretching a long way down the wall.
Also known as a half-beam engine, named for the characteristic nodding action of the overhead beam which is supposed to resemble the action of the rear legs of the grasshopper.
The apse at the west end of the nave in a double-ended church.
A float or pontoon supporting a flexible steel roadway as part of a Mulberry Harbour.
A window either in the shape of a four leaf clover or circular with four foils enclosed.
A room frequently in a military building where sentries guard and protect the premises.
A colonnade all around the exterior of a building or an interior space such as a courtyard.
A rafter set diagonally where two sloping rooves meet.
The west end of a church made up of a tower, entrance hall and upper room, usually with an altar.
A narrow hand loom.
A two dimensional image using paint applied to the surface of a ceiling.
A room within a building, such as a shop or leisure complex,sometimes equipped with cubicles or screens to allow a degree of privacy, where clothing can be tried on or changed.
A wall, usually perforated to allow free passage of air, supporting the ground-floor joists of a building.
The horizontal piece of timber or stone forming the bottom of a window, doorway or other opening, usually and properly designed to throw off water.
A decorated screen behind an altar.
A recess for holding the Holy Sacrament.
Swimming pool, usually part of a larger LIDO or SPORTS CENTRE complex. Medium size pool, between large pool and paddling pool, used primarily for learners.
A brace, parallel to another, in wall framing or arcading, or in trusses between post and tiebeam; also referred to as a duplicate brace.
A roof featuring a covering of straw, reeds, rushes etc. used extensively during the medieval period but also popular in rural or rustic architecture.
A steam engine working at a fixed location.
Term used to indicate the basic unit of accommodation in the plan of a building. It does not necessarily relate to the structural divisions within a building.
A two dimensional image using paint applied to the surface of a wall.
A two dimensional image using paint applied to plasterwork whilst the plaster is still damp.
A moulded bar or baton dividing the panes of glass within a window light.
A wool combing machine for very fine wools.
An arch of the Tuscan Order.
Two buttresses which meet at a right angle at the corner of the structure they are supporting.
An open floor surface for dancing.
A niche in the corner of a structure.
A thin timber, usually sloping on both sides, fixed to the floor between the jambs of a doorway.
A compound steam engine with provision for bleeding off some steam after it has passed through the high pressure cylinder and before it enters the low pressure cylinder.
A capital with an abacus, ovolo, neck and astragal.
A channel that returns water that has been used to turn a waterwheel back to the river or source from which it came.
A small window placed to cast light upon a fireplace.
A lancet arch designed in the composite style.
A room intended for the purpose of teaching a group or class of students simultaneously.
A room within a school, private house or institution used for lessons.
A room in a monastery from which alms were distributed; or the residence of an almoner.
A joint used to secure the end of a joist
An area or series of tiles applied, usually to an interior floor or wall's surface, for practical or decorative effect.
A priest's private entrance into a church, usually found on the south side of the chancel.
The part of a house frequented by servants when not performing their duties.
A horizontal member supporting the posts in a timber framed structure.
Decorative motif in the form of an ox skull.
A wooden, metal or stone strip attached to the floor between the door posts in a doorway covering the joint between two floor surfaces.
A raised platform at the end of a hall on which the high table sat, though sometimes used to describe the table itself.
Paper, often decorated with a printed pattern, painted or embossed design, and usually provided in rolls and used to line the interior walls of a building for decorative purposes.

A waterwheel in which the incoming water is directed onto the periphery at approximately the same level as the axle, the water being kept in contact with the floats, or buckets, by breastwork of masonry, timber or iron.
A passage that runs axially across the end of a hall with a screen between it and the hall and doorways to service rooms on the other side.
A short timber connecting a cruck blade to a wall above the point at which the blade diverges from it.
A high tower, attached to or detached from a building such as a church, with a belfry stage containing bells.
A protective covering applied to the top of a wall, chimney, gable etc., usually curved, sloping or ridged to prevent rainwater settling.
A treasury, often the bursar's room in a college or university.
A recess within a wall or room.
A tall, narrow window with a pointed arched head.
The corner upright bar of a polygonal sash.
A transverse timber resembling a hammer beam but without a hammer post above, being braced to a principal or collar instead.
A housing, normally of triangular section, in a post to take the end of a prop, to hold the post upright during construction.

A device, usually cast iron and often decorative in design, placed upon, embedded in or built into a doostep facilitating the removal of mud, dirt, manure etc. from footwear before entering a building.
A window with a frame for holding the glass, capable of being raised and lowered in vertical grooves.
A beam steam engine originating in Cornwall, first used for raising water from mines.
An arched, or bow-shaped, segmental timber truss with its ends tied together by a straight or cambered member.
A bowstring truss with the space between its members filled by a lattice of crossing diagonal rods.
A roof in which pairs of rafters are fixed to the ridge piece and the wall plates without any collars or tiebeams.
A pilaster without base or capital.
A subsidiary tower or projection attached to the front of a keep containing access to its first floor entrance.
A Byzantine bell-type capital, ornamented with carving resembling wicker-work or basket-weave.
A compact design of steam engine which eliminates a connecting rod by having the piston rod directly attached to the crank.
Bases of this order are often referred to as spira and have a double astragal in the middle.

A room in the house of a Justice of the Peace in which cases were heard. Houses after 1848 contained these for prestige purposes since the practice of hearing cases at home was made illegal at that date.
A flat, circular stone, usually made of sandstone, used in pairs for grinding corn and other meal.
A baluster, circular in plan, turned and cut on a lathe.
A room in which cloaks, coats, hats etc may be left and often including coathooks, hat boxes and similar fixtures and fittings.
A truss with transverse timbers, like a tie-beam from which the middle section has been removed, supported on braces and carrying hammer-posts and braces that carry the open structure of the roof.
A machine for combing out wool, cleaning out extraneous matter and short fibres and laying the combed long fibres more or less parallel to each other as required for Worsted spinning.
A steam engine driving a reciprocating air pump to provide air for a blast furnace.
A type of Ionic diagonal capital where all four sides are the same and with therefore eight volutes.
An inclined wooden trough which channels grain evenly from the feed hopper into the eye of the stones in a corn mill.
A short timber linking two pieces of timber.
A truss with paired vertical posts set on the tie-beam and supporting collar purlins or plates.
A transverse timber in aisled construction, joining aisle posts below tiebeam level, to allow rearing of pairs of posts.
A truss with a transverse timber like a hammer-beam, but braced to a principal or collar without a hammer-post.
A panel of often decorated wrought-ironwork forming the arch between two gate-piers or standards or above a gate. Also used for an arched support for a lamp-holder between two standards.
Man made structure to contain tidal waters, often part of a LIDO, bathing or swimming complex at the sea edge.
An inwards, radial flow reaction water turbine developed by Jean Victor Poncelet (1788-1867) in France in 1826.
The end of a timber post made wider than the rest of its length on one face to accomodate tiebeams, wall-plates etc..
In timber framed buildings this is the division of a wall into narrow panels by vertical studs.
A pad on which a post rests to support part of a structure.

A flour milling machine which uses horizontal steel rollers and dressing machines � seives and purifiers � to gradually reduce wheat grains into constituent parts or �streams� which millers call a �gradual reduction� process.
A room equipped with apparatus to facilitate physical exercise, gymnastics or indoor sports.

An enclosed feature, usually sub-terranean, constructed to contain water for public or private recreation and sporting activities. Often a component of a larger SPORTS CENTRE. Use OUTDOOR SWIMMING POOL for unenclosed pools.
A transverse vestibule at the west end of a church.
A beam the end of which is fixed beyond the upright it passes through.
An arch whose purpose is to arrest or prevent structural movement in a building.
A room that serves as a combined living-room, kitchen and workshop.
A curving, upright timber brace used in roof construction, found in pairs, forming an arch.
A roof consisting of trusses that have a pair of brackets supporting the hammer posts that in turn support the rafters.
A channel or casing containing and protecting wiring, piping etc.
The architectural element made of brick, tile, stone or certain woods which surrounds a fireplace to provide aesthetic and safety benefits.
A structure used for the secure storage of bicycles.
A whole male figure used in place of a column to support an entablature.
A chamber usually used for sleeping.
An arch formed around two arcs whose radii are each equal to the span of the arch.
An apartment for the use of people of high status, often the owner of the building. Also a room adjoining, or close to, a hall and used as a drawing room or venue for private functions.
A roof whose ends are partly gabled and partly sloped.
The hall before a great hall.
The part of a rib or arch visible below the general surface of the vault.
A narrow aisle that pierces an internal buttress.
An apartment or suite of rooms usually occupying the top floor of a building.

Staircase whose moving steps are driven mechanically whilst remaining permanently in a horizontal plane allowing users or objects to be moved effortlessly from one level to another, within or outside a building.
A vault whose bay is divided by diagonal and transverse ribs into six compartments or cells.
A wall mounted, hydraulically operated crane or winch, sometimes used to operate lifts.
A thin, rectangular panel, sometimes enriched with sculptural relief, placed between the triglyphs in a Doric Order frieze.
A short timber supporting end of joists or rafters to facilitate an opening, such as a door or dormer.
An entablature with a plain architrave or principal beam over which are the frieze and cornice.
An arch consisting of a segment of a circle connected to other segments with smaller radii.
The junction of two timbers at right angles, with chamfers or mouldings meeting on a diagonal line, where one timber is cut to fit around the chamfer or moulding of the other timber.
A stone ring built to enclose and sometimes revet a burial mound or cairn.
Specialised fermenting vessel consisting of a two-storey system consisting of a shallow chamber approximately 2m high above which is a walled deck.
A decorative style on a block or band from which an arch springs.
A pipe or channel to carry water outwards from a gutter or behind a parapet. If decorated with grotesque heads it is called a gargoyle.
A truss comprising principal rafters and wall-posts secured by knee-braces or sling-braces, but lacking tiebeams.

An entablature often of great magnificence with bead and reel between fasciae of the architrave, frieze ornamented with continuous sculpture and cornice, with ornate coffers and richly carved modillions.
A structure, or building, forming a supplementary and subsidiary part of a main complex.
A door with a planked outer face and spaced horizontal members such as battens or ledges on the inner face.
A small projection designed to allow space for a bed.
An ovolo moulding below the abacus of a Doric or Ionic Capital.
One of a set of windows placed vertically and linked either decoratively or by aprons extending to the lintel below.
A reading desk usually placed near the west end of the choir.
An engine which uses air or the products of combustion as its working medium.
An ornamental moulding whose curved, segmented form resembles that of a scallop shell.
A room over the vestry of a church, often thought to have been where anchorets lived.
A swimmin pool restricted to the use of men only.
A vertical faced projection in the cornice.
In churches a niche, or shelf, within or beside a piscina, or a table for the sacramental elements and vessels. (Source: Pevsner Glossary)
A room containing one or more thrones.
A simple, single cylinder vertical steam engine with its cylinder mounted directly on a baseplate, driving an overhead crankshaft which is supported by two A-shaped frames
The main gearwheel in a water mill, sharing the same shaft as the exterior waterwheel but placed within the mill.
An arch which is half an ellipse.
A piece of cladding in the form of a length of timber fixed horizontally to the building.
A semi-circular window, often decorated and with its glazing bars radiating like the ribs of an opened fan, set above a door.
An S-shaped ornamental bracket with a greater height than projection.
A principal with a curved lower end that continues down the wall for a short distance.
A patented design of twin-cylinder, inverted vertical steam engine, which can be started irrespective of the position of the crank.
A flat, horizontal strip, block or moulding, sometimes decorated, on a facade or interrupting an architectural feature such as a column.
A flat band carrying a motto.
A concave groove or series of grooves.
A part of an arch between the apex and the springer.
A beam steam engine, reciprocating or rotative, powered by two steam cylinders side by side.
An internal door flush with, and decorated to match, the wall it is set in for the purpose of concealment.
A bridle joint which is invisible from all sides of the timber.
One or more arches built across an angle between two walls to support a polygonal or circular structure.
An early type of steam engine, similar to a Cornish Engine, designed by Edward Bull.
A decorative moulding in a diamond shape.
A curved moulding with a sharp edge running along its centre. So named as its profile resembles the keel of a ship.
A room in general use for persons to meet, often found at institutions such as colleges.
A staircase, usually rising through more than one storey, with a space at its centre around which the stair is carried by strings and newels.
A vertical stone mullion or shaft situated in the centre of a wide doorway to support its lintel or tympanum.
A decorative, framed tablet, usually bearing an inscription or coat of arms, in the form of a scroll or curving piece of parchment.
A room in a church to receive the congregation's offerings.
A vertical member standing without support.
The main axle projecting through the front of a windmill on which the sails are mounted.
A decorative piece descending vertically from the bottom of a newel on a staircase, or from the junction of barge boards at the apex of a gable.
Wooden panelling, especially interior wall panelling that rises only to dado height.
An open area within a bank where financial transactions take place.
A grand staircase where the lower flight splits into two upper flights.
A masonry or brickwork structure projecting above a roof and containing several flues.
A fixed basin, containing Holy Water, positioned near the entrance of a church, usually placed within a niche, on a wall or pier, or freestanding upon a pedestal.
A small mobile steam engine.
An arch which has a larger radius on the front face of a wall than on the back.
A joint in which the tenon passes completely through the receiving timber.
A decorative structure of wood stone or metal, incorporating a shelf, surrounding the opening of a fireplace.
A small dome on top of a larger dome, roof etc.
A cantilevered block projecting from the face of a wall and supporting a structure such as a parapet, beam or arch.
A room in which actions are checked, verified and approved.
A yard intended for the purpose of taking exercise, for example within the grounds of a prison.
A roof made up of common and principal rafters.
An underfloor heating system.
A flat arch or lintel with quadrants on the corbels rising from the jambs.
A plain moulding of semi-circular section.
A court in front of a church, usually open.
An oval or circular decorative feature often bearing an illustration or portrait in light relief.
An ornate version of an eight-voluted Ionic capital known as an angular capital or Scamozzi under which are added two tiers of acanthus leaves.
A room for the storage of bread, biscuits, flour etc. Often part of a buttery in medieval times.
An elementary steam engine working on the atmospheric principle; often used downstream of an overshot waterwheel.
A horizontal brace linking tiebeam and wall plate, or adjacent wall plates at the corner of a hipped roof.
A vertical upright on either side of a door.
A vertical timber in a truss, descending from the ridge, and projecting below the lower transverse timber, normally used with scissor beams.
The openings between the upright blocks, or merlons, of a battlement.
One of the rolls forming the sides of an ionic capital, joining the volutes of the front and rear faces.
A structure, part of a structure or area consisting of plain masonry.
A storeroom for valuable goods, such as wine, oil and spices.
Studding in which the studs are at an inclined angle to the posts.
A flat oval or circular ornamental feature decorated in relief, often with flutes or floral motifs.
The flat slab at the top of a capital.
An ornate version of an eight-voluted Ionic capital known as an angular capital or Scamozzi under which are added two tiers of acanthus leaves.
A wooden commode containing earth, which was used to cover and absorb human waste. An alternative, and rival, to the water closet.
A housing for one or more bells, usually gabled or roofed.
A truss with braces crossing and fixed to each other, thus tying a pair of rafters together.
A horizontal iron bar set in a window opening to which a leaded light is fixed.
A pediment with a gap at its apex, sometimes filled with an urn or other decorative feature.
An adjustable, horizontal beam, supporting the vertical stone spindle in a water or windmill, which allows the gap between the grinding stones to be varied.
A small hole filled or vacated by a stake, normally driven into the ground.
A room intended for the taking of refreshments, usually containing a counter from which they are served.
A parallel or axial-flow reaction water turbine invented in France in 1843, in which water descends through fixed curved guide vanes which direct the flow sideways onto curved vanes on the runner.
A joint in which the tenon retains one or more of the original sides of the timber.
A funnel-like container from which grain or other material is fed to a grinding mechanism below.
A joint in which one timber is applied to the surface of the other.
A raised platform, especially in a theatre or auditorium, for the use of performers or speakers etc.
A darkened room or workshop used by photographers to process prints from glass plates or photographic film.
A casement, usually wooden, in a mill enclosing the grindstones and preventing the loss of ground material before it is channelled into sacks.

A pilaster terminating the side walls of a temple whose capital and base differ from those of its associated columns. Such an arrangement, where the columns do not project beyond the faces of the antae, is said to be 'in antis'.
A pump driven by steam power generated by solid fuels.
An area of a building or complex specifically housing the kitchens, sculleries and services.
Series of rooms in a workhouse complex where children were housed and looked after.
A projecting spout to throw water from a gutter away from the wall, frequently decorated with grotesque figures to spew out evil away from the House of God.

An enclosed structure of brick, stone or metal in which material is heated or burned, or, in a domestic context, food is cooked, through the continuous radiation of heat from its floor, walls or ceiling.
A board on the lower edge of a sloping roof which protects the top of the external wall.
The edge of a roof that hangs over the wall.
An early steam engine using steam at atmospheric pressure. Chiefly employed in mine pumping. sometimes known as a vacuum engine.
A capital which is circular and has several mouldings but no foliage.
A classical order in which the columns or pilasters are applied to a wall.
A very short brace between post and tiebeam, or post and wall plate.
A small convex moulding, often decorated with beads and reels.
A passage between two rooms in a house or leading from the door to the hall.
A staircase normally used by servants and tradesmen.
A plain, narrow moulding or area, sometimes projecting, separating two decorated mouldings or features such as the vertical grooves on a fluted column.
An area of a building or complex, such as a workhouse, hospital or prison used to house infirm inmates suffering from fever.
A water-drain connected to a stone bowl within a niche in the south wall of a chapel, used to receive the water in which the priest washed his hands and rinsed the Chalice.
A duct or channel for hot air to travel through.
A roof consisting entirely of common rafters, without the use of principals, in pairs but not connected by collar-beams.

A capital with two rows of eight acanthus leaves rising above an astragal;from between the leaves of the upper row rise eight stalks,each surmounted by a calyx from which emerge volutes carryingthe corners of the concave sided abacus and central ornaments
A window usually spanning the full width of the north side of a roof allowing the maximum amount of direct and indirect light to enter.
A tiebeam whose central section is omitted to increase head room; its inner ends are held by a slip-braces or struts rising from a dropped tiebeam and can be used in end frames.
A window with a single mullion and a single transom.
An extension to a building usually made primarily of glass, for growing plants.
A small room used as a study for the preservation of papers etc. Generally use for any small room appended to a larger one.
A beam that is part of a frame in a wall.
A circular or oval window or panel. Also the circular opening at the top of a dome.
The structure across the lower end of a medieval hall containing the spere, its supporting posts and tie-beam.
The equivalent of a colonnade but using pilasters.
A diagonal brace running from an end of a cross tree to the vertical post to support this.
A musical instrument that produces sound through the passage of air through a number of pipes controlled by stops, pedals and keyboards.
A decorative moulding cosisting of two ogee-forms with joined faces.
A decorative, spiral scroll form, thought to have been based upon the shape of a ram's horn, employed in consoles, modillions, the capitals of orders such as the Ionic, Composite and Corinthian etc..
An early power loom in which the shedding actions of the machine were controlled by tappets, or wipers, which received their motion from a rotating cam shaft positioned at one side of the loom.
A window which has its head beam decorated with elements of tracery.
A thin masonry facing or cladding employed to conceal the surface or structure behind it.
A purlin that sits in a trench cut in the back of a principal rafter.
A communal sleeping room in medieval houses for male servants.
A device, monogram or symbol incised in stonework by a mason as a means of identifying his work. Particularly common in Romanesque and Gothic buildings.
A ridged roof that slopes on all four sides.
An area or series of tiles applied, usually to an interior floor or wall's surface, for practical or decorative effect.
A steeply pitched spire whose base is significantly narrower than the tower on which it sits.
A recess, usually situated in the north wall of a chancel, often canopied, containing a decorated tomb chest representing the burial and resurrection of Christ.

A sculpture, drawing, or other representation of a face surrounded by or made from leaves and branches or vines. Green Men are frequently found on carvings in both secular and church buildings, and are though to represent pagan fertility deities.
A pier made up of several shafts against it. The shafts can be attached or detached.
Niche intended for pigeons or other birds to nest in, allowing the squabs and eggs to be harvested. Found inside specially built dovecotes and in the walls of other buildings.
A projecting spout to throw water from a gutter away from the wall, frequently decorated with grotesque figures to spew out evil away from the House of God.
A Classical convex moulding, often decorated with egg-and-dart or similar motifs.

An apparatus, featuring an ascending and descending platform or compartment, for carrying people or objects from one level or floor to another. This term can include the shaft or well in which such an apparatus operates.
An arcaded upper aisle, wall passage or blind arcade, usually occurring in large Romanesque or Gothic churches, above the aisle and below the clerestory.

A platform projecting from the surface of a wall of a building carried on brackets, consoles or columns, usually placed before windows or openings, and protected by a railing or balustrade. It can also be a gallery in an auditorium.
A space usually in front of a church entrance but can include the space surrounding a church.
A pump used to move water.
A type of water pump used in the Wigan coalfields in the late 18th and 19th centuries.
The sill beam of the jettied upper floor resting on projecting ends of joists or on jetty brackets.

An enclosed feature, usually sub-terranean, constructed to contain water for public or private recreation and sporting activities. Often a component of a larger SPORTS CENTRE. Use OUTDOOR SWIMMING POOL for unenclosed pools.
A swimmin pool restricted to the use of men only.
A type of slender support carried on a corbel.

A waterwheel where the incoming supply of water is introduced at the top of the wheel and drops onto the paddles, or buckets, of the wheel which rotates in the reverse direction to that of the incoming flow.

A vault composed of concave cones, inverted and overlaid with ribs that rise from the springing of the vault and diverge equally in different directions, producing an effect like the ribs of an opened fan.
A room set aside for the initial treatment of injuries sustained for example in public amenities such as lidos.

A design of steam engine in which either three cylinders, in the case of vertical engines, or four cylinders on horizontal engines, permit steam to be expanded three times as it passes through the installation.
Timber framed construction where a box of horizontal and vertical members is created to support the roof.
A door made up of vertical planks on the outer face and horizontal planks on the inner face.
Manmade feature to contain water for the purpose of floating miniature boats in it. Different to BOATING LAKE as lake can be naturally occuring park feature and can be used for full sized boats.
A lintel over a door.
A small hinged opening within a large door.
A colonnette set in a corner or angle, such as the meeting of two walls or the place at which a doorframe meets the wall it is set in.
An opening in a wall that is usually narrow on the outside but widens internally to allow a defender to stand and shoot through the loophole.
A loft or gallery supported on a covered, vaulted structure and projecting over the rood screen proper, occassionally used by minstrels or singers.
A rectangular dripstone.
A vertical timber set on a collar or tie-beam extending to the apex of a pitched roof.
A beam often situated above, and spanning the width of, the west end of a chancel and supporting a cross or crucifix.
The under surface of an arch, lintel or projecting architectural feature.
A pedestrian passageway, often elevated, connecting two buildings or sections within a building.
A large approximately triangular stone at the foot of a gable, cut to have a horizontal bed and a top conforming, wholly or in part, to the slope of the gable.
The wall plate of the lower storey on which the joists of a jetty rest.
An upright timber standing on a tiebeam to support a collar or purlin. Once used as an alternative to king post, this term is now restricted to the shorter post ending at collar level.
The area directly in front of a fireplace or the floor of a firecplace itself.
A hearth, normally placed clear of the walls, without a chimney stack or hood above.
A long straight timber, halved across other roof timbers, especially running from a wall post or aisle post across the tiebeam to the opposing rafter.
An arch which is formed by keystones but is flat like a lintel
A barrier consisting of a horizontal rail supported by uprights.

A unenclosed feature, usually sub-terranean, constructed to contain water for public or private recreation and sporting activities. Sometimes part of a LIDO complex. Use INDOOR SWIMMING POOL for pools within a covering structure.
An arch whose height is equal to its span.
An iron plate connecting two or more timbers together.

A flour milling machine which uses one or more pairs of horizontal millstones to grind wheat into flour which millers call a �sudden death� process; within not more than 90 seconds complete wheat grain becomes wholemeal flour.
An ornamental panel or decorative structure situated above a mantelpiece
A ward for receiving new paupers upon entry to a workhouse. They had to remain there until checked by medical officer.
A storey of lesser height than those above and below it.
An elevated area or platform in a synagogue where the person reading aloud from the Torah stands.
A hollow moulding, often enriched, similar to a scotia or cavetto, used in Gothic jambs and cornices.
A projecting, sometimes decorated, finish to a moulding, chamfer, label, hood-mould or string-course.
A hand-operated loom on which figured cloth could be woven, also known as a drawing engine.
A hinged, sliding or rolling door placed over a window's exterior or interior.
A roof design, common to weaving sheds, featuring a sawtooth profile with unequal slopes forming each ridge and lights on its northern side.
An inscription or illustration carved into the surface of a stone wall or other architectural element.
A swimming pool restricted to the use of females only.
An arch of the Tuscan Order.
A cruck, forming the main upright of a wall but whose blades are truncated by a transverse member.
A room for detention and imprisonment within a prison.
A room where organizations and associations meet for the undertaking of presentations and consultation.
The face within a pediment, as enclosed by the cornices, or the solid infill of an arch above an impost, sometimes embellished with sculptural or relief ornament.
A dining hall for the use of seasonal labourers.

A classical ornamentation featuring foliage, fruits and, unlike Arabesque, human and animal figures, sometimes fantastic or mythological in nature. The term may have originated from the fact that many examples of such ornamentation were found in grottoes.
An open grating of wrought iron, bronze, wood etc., forming a screen to a door, window or other opening, or used as a divider.
The undressing room in a bath house or similar.
An area of a building functioning as a hospital.
A short timber laid across the top of a masonry wall to carry a wall plate or the foot of a common rafter.
The vertical lining of a doorway, window or other opening.
A room where the public can sit, often in hotels and airports.
The solid, upright part of a battlement separating the openings, or crenelles.
A twin cylinder compound steam engine introduced in the 1870's which combined a horizontal and vertical configuration in the same machine.
An apartment contained, wholly or partially, within the roof space of a house.
A roofed structure, open on one side, usually attached to a building, providing a seating area and its occupants with an uninterrupted exterior view.
A type of steam engine in which the steam cylinders are placed above the crankshaft supported on a robust frame.
A ward in a workhouse for vagrants
A screen, with a wide central opening, or multiple narrower openings, spanning the lower end of a medieval hall, screening the cross entry and defining the screen, or cross, passage.
The passage, area, lobby, room or vestibule into which a building's main entrance opens.
A door divided horizontally into two parts, independently hinged.
A panel of often decorated wrought-ironwork forming the arch between two gate-piers or standards or above a gate. Also used for an arched support for a lamp-holder between two standards.
A flour milling machine using both stones and rollers to produce flour and, perhaps mainly from the stones, animal feed from grains other than wheat.
An arch built by corbelling or cantilevering in from both sides with horizontal joints.
A slope, usually with rails, along which a vehicle can be moved between two relatively flat stretches of railway.
A shortened common rafter that runs between ridge and a valley rafter, or between eaves and a hip rafter.
An arched half-rib applied to the lateral wall surface in a vaulted bay, its function, rather than structural, being to complete the pattern of the vault's main ribs.
A type of direct-acting, twin cylinder steam driven water pump.
A colonnade where it is exposed to the air on two sides.
A pediment with a gap at the centre of its base.
A grooved block placed in series, alternating with the metopes, in a Doric frieze.
A room or group of rooms in a building.
A buttress that is set slightly away from a corner. Usually one of a pair.
Classical decorative motif of interlocking right-angled and vertical lines, usually applied as a continuous pattern.
